title: A Cost-Effective MES-Integrated Data Acquisition Framework for TIG Welding Operations
authors: Gökhan Yenikaya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Manufacturing and Automation (IMA)
abstract: This study introduces a non-intrusive, data-acquisition and monitoring architecture designed for manual TIG welding stations. The system integrates three communication layers—CAN, Modbus/RS-232, and Ethernet—to achieve full traceability of welding and operator events without modifying the original control structure of the power source. A high-impedance, galvanically isolated CAN interface passively captures current, voltage, and operational flags directly from the welding unit's internal bus. Simultaneously, a Delta HMI terminal provides operator-level interactions such as job initiation, downtime reason entry, and shift reporting through Modbus communication. All data are transmitted to a central database for KPI computation, OEE analysis, and production traceability. The approach enables real-time synchronization between the welding process and the MES layer, bridging legacy TIG systems with Industry 4.0 infrastructures. The monitoring design ensures electrical and protocol isolation, guaranteeing full safety and compliance while delivering high-fidelity process intelligence.

title: 3D Vision-Based Weld Extraction for Sphere-Cylinder Junctions: A Geometric Prior Approach
authors: Weijie Cheng, Xincheng Tian
publish: 2026 International Conference on Robot Perception and Intelligent Control (RPIC)
abstract: Precise weld extraction at sphere-cylinder junctions is vital for pressure vessel fabrication but is complicated by saddle geometries and inherent point cloud flaws. Artifacts such as irregular density and noise frequently result in fragmented trajectories or centerline drift. We address this via a robust pipeline grounded in geometric priors. Our method delineates a candidate joint region using dual-surface residuals from fitted outer walls, utilizing an analytic intersection trajectory to guide the extraction. A distinct feature of our approach is estimating the centerline as a scalar displacement along the synthesized normal, rather than utilizing coordinate averaging. This strategy effectively counters the lateral shift often caused by asymmetric sampling. Validation on scan data confirms the generation of continuous, centered trajectories and clean joint bands. Future efforts will focus on production-line integration, including repeatability assessments and welding qualification.

title: Design of a Delta-Sigma Modulator for Biomedical Signal Acquisition Applications
authors: Jingtong Lin
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Electronic Technology, Computers and Software Engineering (AETCSE)
abstract: A high-precision, energy-efficient Delta-Sigma modulator is presented for biomedical signal acquisition. The design employs a third-order CIFF architecture with 4-bit quantization, achieving high-order noise shaping while maintaining system stability. An innovative summing technique based on a two-stage floating inverter amplifier (FIA) is introduced to minimize power consumption, while a switched-capacitor charge-accumulation comparator is utilized to reduce mismatch errors. Implemented in SMIC 180 nm CMOS technology, the modulator achieves an SNDR of 104.49 dB over an 8 kHz bandwidth with a 1.8 V supply. With a total analog power consumption of only 600 μW and a Figure of Merit (FOM<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">SNDR</inf>) of 176.2 dB, the design demonstrates competitive efficiency and precision suitable for biopotential acquisition systems.

title: An Improved YOLOv8n-Based Algorithm for Detecting Surface Welding Parts of Small Balancing Beams of Quay Crane
authors: Ruiqi Su, Chuancun Wang, Long Liu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Robot Perception and Intelligent Control (RPIC)
abstract: To address the problem of mechanical arm spray guns colliding with surface welding parts during the spraying operation of small balance beams for gantry cranes, this paper proposes an improved model, YOLOv8n-CAW, based on YOLOv8n. Firstly, a dual-channel CBAM mechanism is embedded to enhance the perception of welding part features; secondly, the ASFF module is introduced to optimize multi-scale feature fusion through adaptive weighting, eliminating feature inconsistencies; finally, the WIoU loss function is adopted to balance the sample weights, improving the model's generalization ability and robustness. Through multiple rounds of experiments, the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{m A P} \boldsymbol{@} 0.5$</tex> value of YOLOv8n-CAW on the self-built dataset increased from 88.2% to 91.3%, an increase of 3.1 percentage points, meeting the safety requirements of real-time and high-precision detection of welding parts by the mechanical arm spray gun.

title: Structural Topology Optimization Under Self-Weight Loads via Polynomial Stiffness Fitting
authors: Yanyan Zhao
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Manufacturing and Automation (IMA)
abstract: Structural topology optimization under self-weight loads is a typical design-dependent load problem. When solving such problems, the traditional SIMP material model often suffers from parasitic effects in gray (intermediate density) elements. This paper aims to investigate the influence of material interpolation schemes on self-weight optimization within density-based frameworks and provide reasonable recommendations for model selection. To address the parasitic issue, the stiffness curves of intermediate density materials are evaluated using the homogenization method, and a polynomial material interpolation model is constructed via curve fitting. The Method of Moving Asymptotes (MMA) is adopted for the optimization process. Furthermore, to prevent numerical instability associated with the lower bound of density, explicit lower bound constraints are incorporated into the optimization formulation. Finally, the proposed method is validated through several classic numerical examples. The results demonstrate that the proposed model effectively eliminates the parasitic effect of gray materials, thereby verifying the rationality and effectiveness of the method.

title: Fault Detection of UUV Structures Based on EEMD Feature Decomposition and K-Medoids Clustering Algorithm
authors: Bowen Li, Kunlong Hong, Jianzhong Tang, Yong Nie
publish: 2026 International Conference on Robot Perception and Intelligent Control (RPIC)
abstract: Unmanned underwater vehicles (UUVs) operate in complex environments for prolonged periods, during which their structural state is highly susceptible to the effects of external loads and internal dynamic systems, resulting in abnormal vibrations and deformations that may lead to faults. To address the limitations of traditional methods—such as inadequate feature extraction and insufficient anomaly detection accuracy when dealing with nonlinear and non-stationary structural signals, this paper proposes a novel structural fault detection algorithm based on Ensemble Empirical Mode Decomposition (EEMD) and K-medoids clustering. First, EEMD is employed to decompose the original structural signals and extract multi-scale intrinsic mode function (IMF) feature vectors. Subsequently, an autoencoder is used to perform dimensionality reduction on the high-dimensional IMF features, significantly enhancing feature representation capability. Finally, K-medoids clustering is applied to the reduced features for unsupervised clustering and anomaly score computation, thereby achieving accurate detection of structural faults. Experiments conducted under various real-world UUV fault conditions demonstrate that the proposed method effectively distinguishes between normal and abnormal states, exhibiting high detection accuracy and robustness. This approach provides a novel technical pathway for structural health monitoring and fault diagnosis of unmanned vehicles.

title: Accurate Edge Detection Leveraging Biologically Inspired Neural Networks
authors: Yugal Kishore Maheshwari, Danyal Maheshwari, Mousa Karimi
publish: 2025 8th International MultiTopic ICT Conference (IMTIC)
abstract: This study introduces a novel approach for the edge detection task. We propose edge detection based on a biologically inspired neural network, also known as spiking neural network (SNN) using Leaky Integrate and Fire (LIF) neuron model. SNN is an emerging paradigm in computing attempts to mimic the biological functions of the human brain. The main objective of this research is to detect qualitative, clear, and bold edges of given input image using computational efficient SNN algorithm. To realize our approach, the initial stage involves selecting a random image and converted into specific size and into gray scale. Next, receptive fields are used to extract the vertical and horizontal edges and then spiking model is used to transform these edges (data) into spikes and integrate the edges at the output neuron, at last stage an edge detected input image is reformed from trains of spikes at output neuron. A python environment from scratch is used to accomplish this project task. A 5*5 rectangular receptive field is used along with 120 Hz rate encoding frequency to achieve the resultant edges. The obtained images are compared with the Sobel edge detection method as proof of concept of our proposed method and quality of resultant images.

title: Detecting Regulatory Conflicts in Global AI Governance: A Knowledge Graph Approach to Mapping Legal Risks Across Jurisdictions
authors: Li Hanbing
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing (ICONIC)
abstract: The regulating environment in artificial intelligence (AI) is becoming more diverse, with jurisdictions implementing varying regulatory frameworks, posing challenges to multinational organisations. This study aims to investigate the regulatory conflicts in global AI governance and establish a systematic approach to mapping legal risks linked to these conflicts. The methodology employs a knowledge graph-based approach, using real-world regulatory sources like the EU AI Act and simulated regulatory provisions to identify cross-jurisdictional conflicts and assess the legal risks. The dataset includes $\mathbf{2, 4 0 0}$ regulatory provisions and $\mathbf{1, 5 5 2}$ conflict relationships, forming a knowledge graph with $\mathbf{2, 4 8 3}$ nodes and 13,552 edges. The analysis reveals significant conflicts in areas such as incident reporting and model documentation, highlighting regulatory overlaps, ambiguity, and partial conflicts. The study concludes that regulatory fragmentation increases compliance complexity in global AI deployment and recommends that policymakers focus on harmonising key regulatory areas, such as incident reporting and data governance. The findings have practical implications for policymakers and organisations, offering a framework to navigate international AI regulations. However, limitations include the reliance on simulated data, which may not fully represent the complexities of real-world legal systems.

title: A Universal Quantum Gradient Descent Framework for Exact Cover Problems
authors: Jia-Cheng Huo, Ru Zhang, Cong Cao
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Electronic Technology, Computers and Software Engineering (AETCSE)
abstract: Combinatorial optimization problems are foundational in computational science, yet their NP-complete nature renders them classically intractable as problem scales increase. Many such problems can be successfully mapped onto the ground-state search of an Ising Hamiltonian. In this work, we propose a full-quantum iterative algorithm based on the quantum gradient descent (QGD) framework, implemented via the linear combination of unitaries, to solve the standard exact cover problem. By reformulating discrete logical constraints into a quadratic unconstrained binary optimization model, the algorithm evolves an initial uniform superposition state directly within the Hilbert space toward the ground-state subspace. Numerical simulations on instances ranging from different qubits demonstrate that the algorithm achieves high-fidelity convergence with a logarithmic scaling of iteration depth relative to solution precision. Notably, our results reveal that the QGD framework effectively preserves the symmetry of the solution space, allowing for the simultaneous identification of the entire set of optimal results in the presence of ground-state degeneracy. By bypassing complex classical-quantum optimization loops and the associated barren plateau problem, this work provides a robust and scalable full-quantum pathway for resolving high-dimensional logic constraints on future universal quantum computing platforms.

title: FPGA-Based System Communication via Pmod ESP32 Wi-Fi Module
authors: Prabhu E, Rahul C M, Abhishek B Y V, Gajan S, Krishna Prasad C M
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: In this work, a demonstration of Universal Asynchronous Receiver Transmitter (UART) serial communication between the PMOD ESP32 Wi-Fi module and FPGA development boards is explored. The PMOD ESP32 Wi-Fi module has an ESP-WROOM-32 microcontroller in it, which is an integrated BT + WiFi SoC. The main objective is to establish serial communication between the Basys 3 and PYNQ Z1 boards and the PMOD ESP32 Wi-Fi module, enabling wireless connectivity through the WiFi network. UART transmitter and receiver modules are written, synthesized, and implemented on the Basys 3 using Xilinx Vivado, responsible for framing data, checking parity, and synchronizing baud rates. Real-time communication is validated through simulation waveforms and serial terminals including Putty and TeraTerm, and this process is extended to the PYNQ Z1 board. The UART module on PYNQ Z1 is designed using Vivado Block Design with AXI UartLite IP and Zynq Processor IP, and the generated bitstream is used by Python scripts in the internal Jupyter notebook to establish UART communication. The ESP-IDF framework is used to configure the PMOD ESP32 Wi-Fi module for serial communication and wireless broadcasting. These approaches provide insights on HDL-based UART design and Python-based control, demonstrating both traditional RTL FPGA design with Basys 3 and SoC-based FPGA platforms like PYNQ Z1, and can be scaled to IoT applications like home automation and Embedded Systems.

title: Exploring Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks for 2D Human Pose Estimation using Synthetic Data
authors: G Sakthi Priya, K. Vijayalakshmi, K. Vignesh Saravanan, P. Deepthi Pon Gayathri, B. Siva Sundari, T. Vijayalakshmi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Future and Advanced Computing Technologies (ICFACT)
abstract: Human pose estimation is designed to recognize the space position of body joints and the interpretation of human pose in the images or videos, which can be used in various fields such as medical care, sports and more importantly human and computer interface. Nonetheless, quantum machine learning has yet to be applied to large-scale vision data, and the available quantum hardware and simulators are not yet sufficient, such as limited access to qubits and the computational complexity of encoding the content of complex images. To combat these issues, this paper suggests a hybrid quantum-classical architecture to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 D$</tex> human pose that was tested on a synthetic dataset under control. The artificialized data make it possible to systematically experiment but this is not as expensive as working with large real-world datasets. Under the scheme, the problem of encoding normalized image data with amplitude encoding is transformed into a quantum state and pose-related features are produced with a parameterized quantum convolution layer that is executed on a variational quantum circuit that takes advantage of quantum superposition and entanglement. The resulting quantum features are measured and inputted into a classical dense regression network to estimate body joint coordinates. Experimental findings indicate that the hybrid model has the capacity to predict spatial correlations of joints and has a better localization accuracy relative to a classical baseline. These results are an evidence-of-concept demonstration of quantum-assisted feature extraction on structured vision tasks including human pose estimation and indicate the promise of hybrid quantum-classical learning architectures in future computer vision applications.

title: Identification of Non-Fatal Skin Diseases using Deep Learning
authors: Lijetha C Jaffrin, Gunabushan V G, Dheeraj S
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: Non-fatal dermatologic diseases Mpox, which stands for Monkeypox, is a viral disease that has both animal and human forms of transmission and appears through a variety of skin presentations that often resemble a number of different dermatological skin processes. Therefore, accurately diagnosing Mpox at an early date can be very difficult because many types of skin infections can cause similar skin alterations associated with Mpox. Early diagnosis and treatment of Mpox is extremely important to help reduce its transmission to additional humans and provide clinicians with the appropriate resources for early treatment of individuals diagnosed with Mpox. this paper address automating the process of diagnosing Mpox on dermatological images using deep learning. A dataset of both Mpox and nonMpox skin lesions is collected, evaluated and pre-processed to allow for normalizing all of the images and removing any variability before evaluating a group of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) including VGG16, ResNet50, InceptionV3, and EfficientNetB0 and establishing performance characteristics of each of these CNNs compared to an alternative of making predictions using an AlexNet-based CNN. All performance characteristics of the four CNNs will be determined based upon their accuracy rate, precision rate, recall rate, and F1-score. The experimental results indicate that the AlexNet-based CNN was able to perform relatively better than the baseline CNNs at a 91% accuracy and provided an equivalent precision and recall. Therefore, the results of this research highlight that deep learning and using the processes of image analysis will be a valuable tool in supporting healthcare provider decisions on assessing Mpox in clinical scenarios [1].

title: HG-CL: Heterogeneous Graph Contrastive Learning for Encrypted Malicious Traffic Detection with Scarce Labels
authors: Xiangyu Wang, Bin Lu, Qingzhou Yang, Yuji Yang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Electronic Technology, Computers and Software Engineering (AETCSE)
abstract: The widespread adoption of encrypted protocols has rendered traditional deep packet inspection (DPI) techniques ineffective for identifying malicious traffic, while the prohibitive cost of expert annotation leads to a severe scarcity of labeled data in real-world network environments. Existing detection methods predominantly operate within single-flow feature spaces and rely on sufficient labeled samples, failing to exploit the rich structural relationships among network entities such as IP addresses, ports, and TLS certificates. To address these challenges, this paper proposes HG-CL, a heterogeneous graph contrastive learning framework for encrypted malicious traffic detection under label-scarce scenarios. HG-CL constructs network traffic within each time window as a heterogeneous traffic graph comprising flow nodes, IP nodes, and TLS certificate nodes, and employs a dual-view contrastive learning architecture: the network schema view encoder aggregates direct heterogeneous neighbor information through type-aware multi-head attention mechanisms, while the meta-path view encoder captures high-order flow-to-flow semantic correlations via GCN and semantic-level attention. The two views are jointly optimized through a cross-view InfoNCE contrastive loss and a supervised classification loss, enabling the model to derive label-independent supervisory signals from the graph topology. Furthermore, a conditional GAN is introduced to generate graph-adaptive hard negative samples, further sharpening inter-class boundaries in the embedding space. Experiments on the CIC-IDS2017 and CIC-DDoS2019 datasets demonstrate that HG-CL achieves Macro F1 scores of 93.42% and 87.90% with only 5% labeled data, and 99.69% and 96.46% at 50% label ratio, respectively. HG-CL outperforms all baseline methods across all label ratio settings, with particularly pronounced advantages under extremely low label ratios.

title: Graph-Theoretic Labeling Architecture for Robust Frequency Management in Evolving Wireless Topologies
authors: Kowsalya.S, S.Buvaneswari, K.Thiyagarajan
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: Contemporary wireless infrastructures face escalating complexities due to volatile network topologies and erratic traffic demands. Traditional frequency allocation protocols often struggle to maintain stability amidst rapid node mobility and heterogeneous signal environments, primarily due to a lack of proactive fault-tolerance mechanisms. This research introduces a sophisticated graph-labeling framework designed specifically to enhance error resilience in such fluid network conditions. By mapping network entities as vertices and signal interferences as weighted edges, the proposed model assigns frequency channels through a distance-constrained labeling process. A pivotal innovation of this framework is its decentralized "localized correction" mechanism; rather than reconfiguring the entire network upon a failure, the system identifies and rectifies spectral conflicts within a specific neighborhood. This targeted approach significantly curtails signaling overhead and prevents cascading network instability. These refined labels are integrated into real-time scheduling policies, fostering equitable resource distribution, minimized latency, and optimized spectral utilization. Grounded in rigorous graph theory, this self-healing methodology provides an adaptive solution for dense and high-mobility deployments. Extensive simulation benchmarks demonstrate that this framework substantially mitigates co-channel interference and delivers superior throughput consistency, positioning it as a vital contributor to the evolution of 5G-Advanced and 6G communication standards.

title: Agriculture Guru: An AI-Based Multilingual Advisory System for Crop Recommendation and Yield Prediction
authors: Siva Shankar P, Nivetha S, Vasantharaj K
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: Unpredictable weather patterns and declining soil health increasingly threaten global food security. Traditional cultivation practices often fail to adapt to rapid environmental change. The AGRICULTURE GURU system is a localized advisory platform designed to assist small-scale farmers by ranking crop suitability and forecasting yields based on site-specific conditions. The system bridges the disconnect between the basic environmental telemetry and field operations decisions using an organized workflow of precision agriculture. The data fusion approach is the strategy used in the system architecture to generate farm-level profile by combining real-time soil telemetry, meteorological predictions, and past farm records. The central variables of input are soil Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P), Potassium (K) and pH, which are processed using a Random Forest classifier. The ensemble-based model is robust to environmental noise and sensitive to the nutrient patterns of growth. Multi-class assessment is conducted so as to determine the best crop choices in different stress conditions. The validation of the experiment was carried out on 1,800 physical soil samples that were taken in arid, temperate and humid regions of the tropical areas. The results demonstrate a recommendation accuracy of 93.6%. The analysis indicates low regression error in yield prediction and the system supports multilingual integration with 22 languages of the region in support of practical deployment is facilitated. The findings confirm the effectiveness of ensemble learning to scalable agricultural decision support in the presence of climatic variable conditions.

title: HEAL-Net: Hybrid Deep Learning for Tooth Abnormality Detection
authors: Hemalatha S, Savaridassan.P
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: Oral diseases are among the most prevalent global health conditions, with panoramic radiography serving as a key diagnostic tool for detecting dental abnormalities. However, manual interpretation is time-consuming and prone to inter-observer variability, while existing deep learning methods based on single-model architectures struggle to simultaneously capture fine-grained local features and global contextual relationships. This limitation is particularly significant due to the multi-scale nature of dental abnormalities, including caries, impacted teeth, and periapical lesions. HEAL-Net, a hybrid deep learning framework for automated tooth abnormality detection in panoramic radiographs, is introduced. The framework integrates ResNet-101 for local feature extraction and Swin Transformer for global context modeling, combined through concatenation-based feature fusion. Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) is applied for image enhancement, and Gorilla Troops Optimization (GTO) is employed for feature selection to reduce redundancy and improve efficiency. Evaluation on the DENTEX dataset comprising 705 images yields a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 0.892, F1-score of 0.874, and Dice coefficient of 0.881, outperforming state-of-the-art models such as YOLOv8, ResNet-101+FPN, and Swin Transformer by 3.5–6.2%. Statistical analysis using paired bootstrap resampling confirms significance (p < 0.01). Additionally, GTO reduces feature dimensionality by 98.5% and improves inference speed by 33.7%. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of hybrid feature learning for accurate and efficient dental image analysis.

title: Topology Aware Wavelet and Deep Learning Framework with Hybrid Bees Bayesian Optimization for Fault Detection, Classification, and Localization in the IEEE-30 Bus Network
authors: Irfan Ali Channa, Ansar Ahmed Gopang, Sher Muhammad Gorar, Sagheer Hussain Meerani, Saifullah Tunio, Wazir Ali, Naveed Mohsin
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Electronic Technology, Computers and Software Engineering (AETCSE)
abstract: This work proposes the fault diagnosis framework of the IEEE-30—bus system using hybrid model of wavelet-based transient analysis and an Improved Convolutional Neural Network (ICNN). The system consists of three important innovations: topology-aware cases fusion to ensure effective localization, a multi-task learning head to predict fault types and locations simultaneously, and an uncertainty-aware decision layer to predict reliably via relay-action gating. A hybrid Bees Algorithm-Bayesian Optimization (BA-BO) algorithm is used to optimize the hyperparameters. With MATLAB/Simulink results, wavelet+ICNN pipeline offers 99.67% testing performance with eleven different fault types. This framework is protection-grade and has been implemented to exhibit high generalization to a wide range of line lengths and flexible run-time operating conditions at the cost of lightweight computational inference.

title: Construction and Practical Effectiveness of Interdisciplinary PBL Scaffolds in Primary Schools Empowered by Digital Intelligence Technology: An Empirical Study Based on the Theme of Water Conservation
authors: Yuandong Sun, Jin Chen, Beiyi Shi
publish: 2026 15th International Conference on Educational and Information Technology (ICEIT)
abstract: To address the problem of superficial technology application of technology in education and to develop interdisciplinary problem-solving skills in primary school students, this study developed and implemented a four-week interdisciplinary project-based learning program titled "Smart Water Guardians" based on the design research method. The study aimed to explore how to design systematic smart scaffolds to support the complete inquiry cycle. The project built a three-layer smart scaffold system for 28 sixth graders, which included a hardware-aware layer (Arduino UNO and sensors), an analytical modeling layer (Arduino IDE programming), and a collaborative argumentation layer (Tencent Meeting, Tencent Docs). By analyzing learning artifacts, observations, questionnaires and interview data using a hybrid approach, the study found that: (1) the scaffold effectively decomposes the cognitive load of complex tasks, allowing students to focus on scientific argumentation and innovative design; (2) Students' data literacy and computational thinking were significantly enhanced in the collection, analysis and visualization based on real data such as shower greywater recycling, toilet flushing and water dispenser waste. (3) Technology-mediated "virtual-real integrated design hearings" significantly promoted students' scientific communication and critical thinking. This study provides a transferable design framework and practice path that follows the cognitive logic of perception, modeling, and argumentation for the deep integration of digital intelligence technology and interdisciplinary PBL at the primary school stage.

title: Synthetic Inertia Support from Variable-Speed Wind Turbines: Comparative Study
authors: Mohamad Hamoud
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Smart Energy and Control Engineering (SECE)
abstract: High penetration of converter-interfaced wind generation reduces the effective rotational inertia of power systems and makes frequency stability more fragile after large disturbances. Variable-speed wind turbines based on doubly-fed induction generators (DFIGs) can partly compensate this loss by providing synthetic inertia and fast frequency support through their control systems. This paper presents an extended summary of ongoing work on synthetic inertia support from a DFIG-based wind farm using three control options: a fixed-gain PI controller acting on frequency deviation, a rate-of-change-of-frequency (RoCoF) based PI controller, and a Virtual Synchronous Machine (VSM) controller that emulates the swing equation. A reducedorder state-space model is used as a MATLAB/Simulink test bench, while a detailed electromagnetic transient model is implemented in PSCAD. Simulation results for large load steps illustrate how each controller shapes the system frequency nadir, RoCoF, and power sharing between the synchronous generator and the wind turbine.

title: Finite Element Simulation of Surge Mechanical Response for Offshore Floating Photovoltaic Modules
authors: Bo Zhou, Qiang Li, Fuyang Liu, Zirong Cao
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Smart Energy and Control Engineering (SECE)
abstract: Offshore floating photovoltaic (PV) power stations face complex combined wind-wave conditions, challenging module structural safety. Current research lacks detailed mechanical analysis at the module level. This study establishes a refined finite element model of a semi-submersible platform, including modules, frames, supports, and floats. Based on GB 50009-2012 and JTS 145-2015, wind and wave loads for a 25-year return period were applied in coupled simulations. Results show that central modules experience higher frame-transmitted pressure than edge modules. Pressure on glass surfaces is more concentrated perpendicular to support beams, reaching 341.9 N under extreme conditions. The findings clarify surge load transmission, supporting anti-surge design, connection optimization, and accelerated testing of offshore PV modules.

title: Assessing Digital Competence in Teacher Preparation: A CIPP-Based Evaluation of the BTLED Curriculum
authors: Frank Aiken O. Alan, Vincent P. Baclayon, Cheniezalyn V. Villamor, Cesar L. Autor
publish: 2026 15th International Conference on Educational and Information Technology (ICEIT)
abstract: This study evaluated the Bachelor of Technology and Livelihood Education (BTLED) program of Caraga State University – Cabadbaran City (CSUCC) using the Context–Input–Process–Product (CIPP) model to determine the extent of digital competence integration within teacher education. Employing a quantitative descriptive research design, data were gathered from students and faculty through a validated researcher-made questionnaire aligned with the four CIPP dimensions. Findings revealed that across the context, input, process, and product domains, the BTLED program achieved a generally effective level of implementation. The program’s curriculum design and faculty qualifications are aligned with institutional goals and Commission on Higher Education (CHED) standards, demonstrating strong pedagogical and professional competence. However, limitations were observed in digital infrastructure, budgetary allocation, and access to instructional technologies. The results highlight the need for sustained institutional investment, faculty digital training, and continuous evaluation mechanisms to achieve sustainable digital transformation. The study concludes that the CIPP model provides a comprehensive framework for assessing and improving digital literacy integration in teacher education, ensuring curriculum responsiveness and educational quality in the context of 21st-century learning.

title: TB-Insight: A Pathology-Aware Temporal AI Framework for Accurate Tuberculosis Identification and Continuous Disease Monitoring
authors: R Prabhu, R Raja Guru, B Mohan, M Lavanya, B Premkumar, T Anjani Devi, K Ramalakshmi
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: Tuberculosis (TB) remains a major global health challenge, necessitating accurate and timely diagnostic tools for early detection and continuous monitoring. This paper presents TB-Insight, a hybrid artificial intelligence framework for tuberculosis identification and disease progression analysis using chest X-ray images. The proposed approach integrates Vision Transformer and convolutional neural network architectures with temporal modeling and federated learning to enable longitudinal monitoring and privacy-preserving deployment. TB-Insight achieves a detection accuracy of 99.82% on chest X-ray datasets and provides clinically interpretable predictions through uncertainty estimation and explainability mechanisms. An edge–cloud deployment framework enables real-time patient risk stratification and alert generation, supporting scalable use in resource-constrained environments. Extensive experimental validation across multiple international datasets demonstrates the robustness and generalizability of the proposed framework for AI-assisted tuberculosis screening and management.

title: An Empirical Study on the Influencing Factors of GenAI on Learning Achievement in Basic Courses of Artificial Intelligence Major
authors: Haibin Xie, Hanyin Zheng, Yijing Huang, Guolin Wu
publish: 2026 15th International Conference on Educational and Information Technology (ICEIT)
abstract: Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) is significantly impacting the learning achievement of emerging engineering majors in programming education. First, a "core-extension" Structural Equation Model (SEM) framework is constructed, which incorporates four progressive core literacies: Knowledge and Skills, Methods and Abilities, Thinking and Innovation, Career Competence Development and two GenAI variables. Secondly, GenAI’s direct and indirect effects on learning ability are empirically examined point to the cultivation path "Knowledge→Methods→Thinking→Career literacy". The results confirm the significance of this progressive literacy path, with Methods and Abilities acting as the critical intermediary; GenAI exerts a direct positive effect on Methods and Abilities and an indirect positive effect on Career Competence Development via literacy chain transmission. Finally, some targeted and operable instructional implications are proposed for the quality assessment and teaching optimization of GenAI-integrated programming courses in emerging engineering education.

title: Research on Security and Stability Issues of Extremely Weak Sending-End Power Grid Under Access of Multi-Terminal UHV Flexible DC
authors: Wenman Gao, Shaoxuan Zhu, Xiangyi Chen, Meng Chen
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Smart Energy and Control Engineering (SECE)
abstract: To address the security and stability risks arising from the integration of multi-terminal UHV flexible DC systems into extremely weak sending-end AC grids, and to solve the stability challenges caused by insufficient rotational inertia, weak voltage support, and control interactions among multi-terminal flexible DC systems in extremely weak grids, this paper combines the technical characteristics of multi-terminal UHV flexible DC and the operation features of extremely weak sending-end grids. It analyzes the core factors affecting the power system security and stability after integration, investigates the characteristics and criteria of different security and stability issues, and conducts case simulation studies. The results show that there exist interactive influences on active and reactive power as well as coupling effects between AC and DC faults in the hybrid system consisting of multi-terminal flexible DC and weak sending-end AC grids. The case simulations demonstrate that generator rotor angle instability occurs after DC blocking faults. The main measure adopted is to advance the action time of the security and stability control system for generator tripping.

title: Sharing but Not Caring: Similar Outcomes for Shared Control and Switching Control in Telepresence-Robot Navigation
authors: Juho Kalliokoski, Evan G. Center, Steven M. LaValle, Timo Ojala, Basak Sakcak
publish: 2025 IEEE Conference on Telepresence
abstract: Telepresence robots enable users to interact with remote environments, but efficient and intuitive navigation remains a challenge. In this work, we developed and evaluated a shared control method, in which the robot navigates autonomously while allowing users to affect the path generation to better suit their needs. We compared this with control switching, where users toggle between direct and automated control. We hypothesized that shared control would maintain efficiency comparable to control switching while potentially reducing user workload. The results of two consecutive user studies (each with final sample of n = 20) showed that shared control does not degrade navigation efficiency, but did not show a significant reduction in task load compared to control switching. Further research is needed to explore the underlying factors that influence user preference and performance in these control systems.

title: MiXR-Intent: Intention Prediction for Immersive Remote Encountered-Type Haptics
authors: N. B. Takele, D. Delehelle, Y. Kim, S. Anastasi, N. Deshpande, J. Ortiz, D. G. Caldwell, C. Recchiuto, Y. T. Tefera
publish: 2025 IEEE Conference on Telepresence
abstract: This paper introduces MiXR-Intent, a deep learning-based system for intention estimation in immersive telepresence with haptic feedback. MiXR-Intent uses gaze direction, upper body posture, and hand movement patterns to accurately predict user intentions and contact points within virtual reality (VR) environments. By interpreting these cues, the system aims to minimize interaction noise and latency—challenges that are especially critical when haptic feedback is employed for remote telepresence and teleoperation applications. In this paper, we propose a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) neural network as a method for predicting user intentions and contact points during interaction. Our approach focuses on using three interaction types: pushing, pointing, and grasping, which could be used as a fundamental interaction types for enabling natural and intuitive user experiences. To improve robustness and generalization in all directions and positions, each interaction type data is collected across six distinct directional movements, to make sure diverse trajectory variations relative to the user’s body. This data variability allows the proposed LSTM neural network to capture user interaction under different spatial positions. The model is trained on a dataset of 6120 samples collected from 20 subjects, using 17 contact points and capturing gaze and upper body movements across six distinct directions. We evaluated the proposed system using various parameters of the LSTM architecture and conducted a performance analysis across multiple metrics, achieving up to 95% accuracy in classification.

title: Gamified Teaching for Marginalized Group Engagement: A Case Study in Early Childhood Education
authors: Shuangqi Li, Li Li
publish: 2026 15th International Conference on Educational and Information Technology (ICEIT)
abstract: This study focuses on the participation dilemmas of marginalized groups in preschool education courses within higher education institutions, with the "Early Childhood Play and Guidance" course as a practical vehicle. It systematically explores the effectiveness of gamified teaching in promoting active participation among these groups. Based on self-determination theory, differentiated instruction theory, flow theory, and inclusive education theory, this research constructs a tripartite gamified teaching model comprising a multi-collaborative participation pathway, a tiered task adaptation system, and a comprehensive incentive evaluation mechanism. Through various methods such as online challenges, offline role-playing tasks, and mixed narrative activities, the model adapts to the diverse needs of marginalized students, including those from different disciplines, introverted individuals, and those with special needs. The practical results indicate that gamified teaching significantly enhances students’ course engagement, mastery of knowledge and skills, and overall competencies, particularly aiding marginalized groups in transitioning from passive observation to active participation. The study recommends strengthening teachers’ gamified teaching capabilities, establishing school-enterprise collaborative practice mechanisms, and further optimizing personalized support and long-term tracking systems. The findings of this research provide innovative practical and theoretical references for the reform of preschool education courses and contribute positively to the international practice of inclusive education concepts.

title:
Ferroelectric/Dielectric Spacer Engineering in Negative capacitance MoS
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    FinFETs: An Insight into Digital, Analog/RF and Circuit Applications
authors: Kotha Venkata Ramakrishna, Sresta Valasa, Narendar Vadthiya
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: This work for the first time examines the idea of replacing the dielectric (DE) spacers with ferroelectric (FE) spacers in the extension areas of MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> based Negative Capacitance (NC) FinFET using a calibrated TCAD setup. Four spacer configurations i.e., both-side air spacers (C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sub>), source-side air with drain-side FE (C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>), source-side FE with drain-side air (C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>), and both-side ferroelectric (FE) spacers (C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sub>) are investigated. Among these, C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sub> and C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> outperforms in terms of digital and analog/RF performance. For C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sub> and C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> configurations an ~2 and ~1 order improvement is observed in terms of I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on</sub>/I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">off</sub> ratio and nearly 1.9 mV/dec change in SS when compared to C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> and C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sub> configurations. This suggests that opting the air spacer gives better digital performance in comparison with other configurations. However, the Negative Differential Resistance (NDR) is also noticed for the same C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sub> and C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> configurations. Further, an improvement of ~90.07 %, ~87.5% in g<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">m</sub> is noticed for C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sub> and C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> when compared to the other two configurations. The gate capacitance (C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">gg</sub>) is reduced for C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sub> boosting the cut-off frequency (f<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">T</sub>) to > 70% when compared to other configurations. To assess the viability of MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> NC FinFET in digital/analog IC applications, a common source (CS) amplifier and resistive load inverter is designed. Overall, these findings contribute to the reference at N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sub> node for spacer engineering optimization in MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> NC-FinFET for commercialization.

title: Expectation-Maximization-Based Data-Driven Predictive Control_supp2-3688263.pdf
authors: Xinpeng Liu
publish: N/A
abstract: Willems' fundamental lemma provides a new paradigm for data-driven system modeling and control by enabling trajectory prediction through learning of system behavior. To mitigate the influence of measurement noises, existing data-driven control approaches mainly incorporate regularization terms into the optimization problem. Although effective to some extent, their efficacy is fundamentally limited, as they do not explicitly address the probabilistic nature of noise nor do they systematically quantify inherent modeling uncertainties. This study proposes a data-driven control method based on the expectation-maximization (EM) framework. This method first establishes a probabilistic model for the offline data and performs data denoising based on a batch EM algorithm. Subsequently, a recursive EM algorithm, integrated within a receding horizon optimization framework, is used to solve the data-driven implicit model. By introducing hidden variables into the probabilistic model, the proposed method effectively addresses optimization problems involving noisy measurements in a unified probabilistic framework. Validation results from numerical simulations, the public NASA JT9D aero-engine system, and a hardware-in-the-loop platform for a two-spool turbofan engine show that, compared with the benchmark subspace predictive control method, the proposed method reduces the root-mean-square error (RMSE) by 30<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula>, 28<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula>, and 30<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> and increases the coefficient of determination (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathrm{R^{2}}$</tex-math></inline-formula>) by 45<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula>, 7<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula>, and 6<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula>, respectively, thus achieving a significant improvement in control performance.

title: DNN-Koopman-MPC Strategy for a Nonuniform Quay Crane: Theory and Experimental
authors: Huapeng Zhang, Wei Xie, Weidong Zhang, Hao Jiang, Bin Zhao
publish: IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
abstract: This article presents a novel real-time trajectory tracking strategy for nonuniform quay crane systems, aiming to suppress container swing and enhance transportation efficiency. The primary contribution of this work is threefold. First, it introduces a deep neural network (DNN)-based framework within the generalized Koopman operator theory to globally linearize the highly nonlinear and nonuniform crane dynamics. This approach overcomes the limitations of traditional local linearization and manual dictionary selection in existing Koopman methods, leading to a more accurate and generalizable linear predictor. Second, a model predictive control (MPC) scheme is developed based on this DNN-Koopman model, enabling multivariable constraint handling for safe and optimal operation. Third, to address the computational challenge of real-time MPC optimization under hardware constraints, the quantum-behaved particle swarm optimization algorithm is integrated, ensuring fast convergence to feasible and near-optimal solutions. The stability of the closed-loop system is rigorously proven using the Lyapunov theory. Extensive hardware experiments on a 1:9.675 scale Shanghai Zhenhua Heavy Industries Company Limited quay crane prototype demonstrate that the proposed DNN-Koopman-MPC strategy achieves rapid and stable trajectory tracking while effectively suppressing swing, even under disturbances.

title: Wind-Driven Regime of GNSS-R Signatures in Northern Lakes for Lake Ice Detection
authors: Zhizhou Guo, Wei Wan, Baojian Liu, Xianci Wan, Jie Zhang, Xiuqing Hu, Huan Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Spaceborne Global Navigation Satellite System Reflectometry (GNSS-R) has emerged as a promising technique for cryospheric monitoring. Lake ice is an essential cryospheric parameter that serves as an indicator of climate variability and change. However, inland lakes are characterized by finite boundaries and complex environmental conditions, which pose significant challenges to lake ice monitoring using GNSS-R. Although several case studies exist, a clear understanding of the physical regimes governing GNSS-R signal differences between lake ice and water remains lacking, thereby limiting scalability to broader applications. In this study, GNSS-R observations from the Fengyun-3E (FY-3E) satellite are analyzed over northern mid- and high-latitude lakes (≥ 40°N). Focusing on the impact of wind forcing over finite inland water bodies, this study systematically examines power- and coherence-based observables alongside environmental conditions across varying distance-to-shore zones to reveal wind-driven signal differentiation patterns and distinct separability regimes. The results clarify the physical origin of near-shore contamination, demonstrating that the reduction of wind-induced surface roughness near the shoreline dominates signal behavior within approximately 5 km. A Random Forest framework is further employed to integrate multi-observable information and quantify variable importance, highlighting the necessity of synergizing coherent reflection metrics with roughness-sensitive observables. It is shown that single observables are prone to ice–water ambiguity under specific wind conditions, whereas multi-observable synergy enables robust discrimination without the need for real-time wind measurements. Overall, this study establishes a physically grounded, wind-driven regime for interpreting GNSS-R signatures over northern lakes and confirms the feasibility of the method for large-scale lake ice monitoring.

title: Unleashing Tool Engineering and Intelligence for Agentic AI in Next-Generation Communication Networks
authors: Yinqiu Liu, Ruichen Zhang, Dusit Niyato, Abbas Jamalipour, Trung Q. Duong, Dong In Kim
publish: IEEE Communications Magazine
abstract: Nowadays, agentic AI is emerging as a transformative paradigm for next-generation communication networks, promising to evolve large language models (LLMs) from passive chatbots into autonomous operators. However, unleashing this potential requires bridging the critical gap between abstract reasoning and physical actuation, a capability we term tool intelligence. In this article, we explore the landscape of tool engineering to empower agentic AI in communications. We first analyze the functionalities of tool intelligence and its effects on communications. We then propose a systematic review for tool engineering, covering the entire lifecycle from tool creation and discovery to selection, learning, and benchmarking. Furthermore, we present a case study on tool-assisted uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAV) trajectory planning to demonstrate the realization of tool intelligence in communications. By introducing a teacher- guided reinforcement learning approach with a feasibility shield, we enable agents to intelligently operate tools. They utilize external tools to eliminate navigational uncertainty while mastering cost-aware scheduling under strict energy constraints. This article aims to provide a roadmap for building the tool-augmented intelligent agents of the 6G era.

title: GNSS Induced Spoofing Detection Based on the Sum of Carrier Phases of Early and Late Branches
authors: Wenyi Wang, Lei Wang
publish: IEEE Communications Letters
abstract: The Early Late Phase (ELP) is a Signal Quality Monitoring (SQM) method for spoofing detection. It detects spoofing by tracking carrier phase inconsistencies between the Early and Late branches during spoofing induction. However, after the spoofing and authentic correlation functions fully separate, the overlay distortion disappears(indicating that the receiver has been successfully spoofed). At this stage, the accumulated frequency offset introduces a common carrier-phase shift in both branches, and the phase difference cancels this offset, degrading ELP performance. To address this issue, an enhanced ELP method is proposed. The key innovation is using the sum of carrier phases from two tracking branches as a new SQM metric. Research shows that this sum exhibits a significant non-zero offset during both the spoofing induction phase and after a successful attack. Consequently, spoofing can be detected continuously and effectively. The proposed method was evaluated using the Texas Spoofing Test Battery (TEXBAT). Compared to traditional SQM metrics, it maintains high sensitivity during induction and remains effective after successful spoofing. Thus, detection efficiency and reliability are significantly improved across various scenarios. However, this method is inapplicable to spoofing scenarios lacking a cumulative carrier phase shift during the induction phase.

title: SPCMap: A Power-Aware and Compilation-Efficient Mapping Framework for Heterogeneous CGRAs in Onboard SAR Imaging
authors: Yongrui Li, Liang Chen, Han Li, Hao Shi, Yizhuang Xie, Chao Wang, Wei Li, Linlin Ge
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Onboard real-time Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imaging is essential for time-sensitive applications but faces strict power constraints on satellites. Heterogeneous Coarse-Grained Reconfigurable Arrays (CGRAs) with circuit-switched networks offer an efficient and flexible platform for SAR computing, yet existing mapping methods fail to jointly optimize power and compilation time efficiency. This paper introduces SPCMap, a SAR-aware mapping framework for heterogeneous CGRAs that tackles this joint challenge. SPCMap features four innovations: (1) a power-aware Modulo Routing Resource Graph (MRRG) modeling method for accurate power estimation; (2) an anchor diffusion method leveraging the multi-source convergence pattern of SAR data flows to prune the search space; (3) a non-greedy progressive search method to escape local optima; and (4) a parameterized active backtracking method for robustness. Evaluated on 13 real SAR Data Flow Graphs (DFGs) using a 12-nm CGRA platform, SPCMap achieves 100% mapping success. It reduces router power by up to 62.7%, with an average deviation of only 2.4% from the theoretical optimum, while keeping compilation time within 1.4× of the fastest greedy baseline. Ablation studies confirm the contribution of each component. In cross-platform comparisons, the proposed CGRA with SPCMap attains a leading normalized energy efficiency of 3.44×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">7</sup> pixel<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>/s/W, outperforming existing processors. SPCMap thus balances power, compilation time, and reliability, providing a practical compilation solution for energy-efficient onboard SAR imaging.

title: A Novel Surrogate Modeling Framework Based on Fast Time-Domain Simulation and Transfer Learning for Signal Integrity Analysis
authors: Meng-Yi Yu, Chang-Sheng Mao, Da-Wei Wang, Wen-Sheng Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology
abstract: In high-speed electronic systems, signal integrity (SI) issues become increasingly prominent as the operating frequencies rise and integration levels deepen, making accurate SI modeling a key requirement. This paper presents a modeling framework that combines fast time-domain simulation with transfer learning to reduce the computational cost of transient and electromagnetic simulations while building high-accuracy surrogate models. In the source domain, circuit simulation along with the multiple edge response method is used to generate large-scale transient waveforms and worst-case eye diagrams for pretraining neural networks. The target domain uses S-parameters extracted from electromagnetic simulation to construct interconnect models, and transient simulation is performed to obtain high-fidelity output responses. Subsequently, deep neural network and temporal convolutional network are used as surrogate models for geometric parameter-to-eye diagram metrics mapping and input-output waveform mapping tasks, respectively, where the eye diagram metrics include eye height, eye width, and jitter peak-to-peak. The knowledge learned from the source domain is transferred to transient simulation data in the target domain using either fine-tuning or partial-freezing strategies, which further enhance the performance under limited data scenarios, thereby validating the framework’s adaptability and robustness across diverse tasks and system configurations.

title: Progressive Orthogonal Multimodal Similarity Learning for Metabolite-Disease Association Prediction
authors: Qiao Ning, Yanpeng Liu, Yuanjie Li, Shaohang Qiao, Lu Jiang, Qian Ma, Pengfei Wang, Hui Li, Shikai Guo
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: The identification of potential associations between metabolites and diseases is crucial for understanding the onset and progression of diseases. Although numerous methods have been developed to predict metabolite-disease associations (MDAs), few methods fully mine the complementary information between different similarity relation or learn higher-order relationships between metabolites or diseases. To address these challenges, we propose a method for MDAs prediction with Progressive Orthogonal Multimodal Similarity Learning (POMSL). POMSL first constructs two hypergraphs within single-modal similarity with KNN and K-means to capture higher-order complex relationships. Then, hierarchical contrastive learning is applied to enhance the consistency of multi-similarity features by performing contrastive learning for inter-similarity and intra-similarity views. Next, a progressive orthogonal multimodal similarity integration strategy is developed to ensure the effective fusion of cross-similarity complementary information and to enhance the divergence of multi-similarity features. Finally, a bilinear decoder is used to predict MDAs. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that POMSL performs excellently in MDA prediction and, more importantly, validate the effectiveness of hierarchical contrastive learning and progressive orthogonal multimodal similarity learning. The source code and dataset are available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/1521250466LYP/POMSL</uri>.

title: An Inductive Multi-Scale Graph Memory Network for Spatial-Temporal Traffic Data Kriging
authors: Adnan Zeb, Pir Masoom Shah, Junde Chen, Peipei Peng, Jianying Zheng
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Complete traffic data collection is essential for the effective operation of intelligent transportation systems. However, deploying sensors at every location for complete coverage is not economically and geographically feasible, resulting in incomplete traffic data. Such sparsity of data adversely affects downstream operations like traffic monitoring and control. To overcome this issue, spatial-temporal kriging methods have recently become popular in inferring missing sensor data from observed locations. However, existing methods are limited in modeling the complex multi-scale spatial dependencies within traffic networks and often fail to effectively retain and leverage historical traffic patterns, which ultimately limits their generalization capabilities to unobserved locations. In this paper, we propose a novel spatial-temporal traffic data kriging model, the Inductive Multi-Scale Graph Memory Network (IMSGMN), which advances beyond state-of-the-arts in both inference capabilities and computational efficiency. Specifically, IMSGMN integrates two innovative components: a Multi-scale Spatial Diffusion (MSD) module that generates and processes multiple dynamic graph structures to capture complex, time-varying, sample-level spatial dependencies at local, intermediate, and global scales, and a Kriging Memory Bank (KMB) that stores and retrieves relevant historical traffic patterns to effectively model wide-range temporal dependencies. A Spatial-Temporal Fusion (STF) module then combines the outputs of both modules to generate the final output. Extensive evaluations on standard traffic datasets demonstrate that IMSGMN achieves significant improvements in interpolation performance while substantially reducing computational time by 70% and parameter count by 54.2% compared to recent state-of-the-art deep learning-based approaches.

title: Model-Free Dynamic Consensus in Multi-Agent Systems: A Q-Function Perspective
authors: Maryam Babazadeh, Naim Bajcinca
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems
abstract: This paper presents a new method for dynamic consensus in linear discrete-time homogeneous multi-agent systems (MAS). Achieving state consensus in such systems involves constraints linked to the graph's spectral properties, complicating the design of coupling gains, especially in large-scale networks. The proposed approach reformulates the dynamic consensus problem with a prescribed convergence rate by introducing a state–action value function within a synthetic linear quadratic regulation (LQR) framework, thereby expressing the problem as a semidefinite program (SDP). The resulting SDP supports the joint design of local feedback and coupling gains in both model-based and model-free settings. To handle non-convex feasibility conditions, a convex–concave decomposition strategy is developed, guaranteeing convergence to a stationary point. In the fully model-free case, the method eliminates the need for system identification or explicit knowledge of agent dynamics, relying solely on input–state data to construct an equivalent data-driven SDP. Finally, a new algorithm balancing feasibility, convergence rate, and energy efficiency enhances design flexibility. Numerical results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in diverse scenarios.

title: DRL-Driven Robust Topology Control and Flow Scheduling for SDN-Enabled Underwater Acoustic Sensor Networks
authors: Yin Wang, Na Xia, Xu Liu
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Node failures in underwater acoustic sensor networks (UASNs), caused by network attacks or energy depletion, can trigger cascading failures and compromise service quality. To address this, we propose a joint optimization scheme to enhance network robustness and prolong network lifetime. Network topology optimization is reformulated as an integer nonlinear problem, and a centralized control framework is developed based on software-defined networking (SDN). Within this framework, a scale-free topology evolution model is introduced to generate the initial topology, aiming to enhance the network’s robustness against random attacks. Subsequently, a robustness enhancement algorithm based on actor–critic deep reinforcement learning is developed for the resulting topology. The agent interacts with the topological environment, learns and evaluates robustness under various topologies, and intelligently adjusts node links to enhance resistance to malicious attacks. Furthermore, we develop an SDN-enabled flow scheduling algorithm that balances traffic over multiple paths by minimizing the maximum energy pressure, thereby maximizing network lifetime. Numerical simulations show that the cascading robustness of our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art schemes, including DDLP, FA-SFTC, ETFLA, and Initial-BA, by 17.88%, 38.6%, 113.62%, and 117.71%, respectively. In terms of network lifetime, our scheme consistently improves upon FA-SFTC, DDLP, ETFLA, FAA, and VODA by 6%, 58.21%, 41.95%, 27.19%, and 149.65%, respectively.

title: Multi-Objective Neural Architecture Search for Cognitive Diagnosis: Balancing Accuracy and Interpretability With Probabilistic Models
authors: Yifei Sun, Mingkai Duan, Sicheng Hou, Shi Cheng, Maoguo Gong, Zhi-Hui Zhan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Cognitive diagnosis (CD) is a fundamental task within computational social systems, essential for personalizing learning in intelligent education by assessing a learner’s fine-grained knowledge proficiency. A critical dilemma exists in designing cognitive diagnosis models (CDMs): conventional psychometric models are interpretable but often simplistic, while complex deep learning models achieve high accuracy at the cost of becoming uninterpretable “opaque models.” This lack of transparency is a major barrier to trust and adoption in real-world educational social systems. To address this challenge, this article introduces probabilistic model-building for cognitive architecture search (PMB-CAS), a novel framework that automates the discovery of CDMs that balance diagnostic accuracy with structural interpretability. We reframe the design process as a multi-objective optimization problem, balancing diagnostic performance [area under the curve (AUC)] with a refined, operator-weighted model interpretability score (MIS) that fairly assesses topological complexity. The framework navigates a flexible, tree-based search space using a probabilistic model-building strategy to efficiently discover promising architectures. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that PMB-CAS discovers a portfolio of Pareto-optimal models that not only achieve state-of-the-art accuracy, outperforming established baselines, but also offer varying degrees of structural transparency. This work provides educators and stakeholders with a spectrum of trustworthy solutions and establishes a new paradigm for developing effective and verifiable models for computational social systems.

title: A Neuromorphic Approach to DDoS Detection: Hardware Accelerator for Integer-Quantized Spiking Neural Networks
authors: Phu Nguyen Phan Hai, Khang Pham Lo Hoang, Cam Doan Ngoc, Bao Bui Quoc, Trang Hoang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks remain a persistent threat to Internet of Things (IoT) and edge networks, where detection systems are often constrained by latency and power budgets. This paper presents a SNN based DDoS detection system that spans packet level preprocessing, integer quantized inference, and FPGA deployment. A learnable leaky integrate and fire (LIF) input encoder with per feature firing thresholds is introduced to transform normalized traffic features into spike trains in a trainable manner. The entire inference pipeline operates in integer arithmetic through quantization aware training, eliminating all floating point operations. A four stage pipelined hardware accelerator is implemented on a Xilinx Zynq UltraScale+ multiprocessor system-on-chip (MPSoC). Experiments on the Bot IoT and ToN IoT datasets demonstrate competitive detection accuracy, and the accelerator achieves an inference latency of 91.58 μs, delivering a 182.1× speedup over the ARM Cortex A53 with 10.95% FPGA logic utilization.

title: Evolution of Layer1/Layer2-Triggered Mobility in 5G-Advanced: From Release 18 Foundation to Release 19 Enhancements and Beyond
authors: Dogukan Atik, Behnam Khodapanah, Sanjay Goyal, Murat Gursu, Jedrzej Stanczak, Anastasios Kakkavas, Jani-Pekka Kainulainen
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This work provides a comprehensive examination of Layer 1/Layer 2-triggered mobility (LTM) enhancements introduced in 3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP) Release (Rel.) 19, extending the foundational framework established in Rel. 18. The key advancement is the extension of LTM to inter-central unit (CU) scenarios, significantly broadening its applicability across diverse network deployments. This extension necessitated comprehensive standardization work addressing security key management, dual connectivity (DC) operations, and channel state information (CSI) resource coordination mechanisms. The measurement capabilities were improved by incorporating channel state information reference signal (CSI-RS)-based layer 1 (L1)-reference signal received power (RSRP) measurements and implementing event-triggered reporting to reduce signaling overhead. Additionally, conditional LTM enhances mobility robustness through autonomous user equipment (UE)-triggered cell switching, while early CSI acquisition procedures optimize post-handover throughput performance. The paper elaborates on the technical implementation of inter-CU LTM, covering network signaling architectures, security framework adaptations, and DC integration aspects. Furthermore, we explore Rel. 20 developments focusing on LTM integration with Carrier Aggregation and adaptive measurement configuration mechanisms, alongside initial considerations for 6G mobility evolution. To emphasize the importance of reducing the handover interruption time, our system-level simulation analysis quantifies the transport layer benefits of reduced interruption times, demonstrating that reducing the interruption duration in LTM to approximately 20 ms delivers significant transmission control protocol (TCP) throughput gains over conventional handover’s 100 ms interruption, especially when TCP re-transmission timeout (RTO) timers are set at 100 ms or below.

title: Optimizing Electric Vehicle Charging Load Forecasting via Ensemble of Diffusion Models
authors: Xijuan Sun, Di Wu, Michael Jenkin, Arnaud Zinflou, Boyu Wang, Benoit Boulet
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Vehicles
abstract: The global adoption of electric vehicles (EVs) has experienced rapid growth in recent years. This has resulted in higher EV charging demands, placing additional pressure and challenges on existing smart grid infrastructure. Accurate and reliable short-term electric vehicle load predictions are essential to optimize smart grid operations, prevent overload of the grid and transformers, and ensure that electricity distribution aligns with actual demand. Although machine learning methods have found wide use in electric vehicle charging load forecasting, they struggle to capture complex feature patterns and accurately quantify uncertainties. In contrast, generative AI has shown great promise in various real-world applications due to its ability to model intricate data distributions and provide robust probabilistic forecasts. In this work, we leverage denoising diffusion probabilistic models, a type of generative model, to address the challenges of probabilistic electric vehicle load forecasting. Specifically, we propose an ensemble of conditional diffusion models as a unified framework to improve forecasting accuracy. We evaluate our approach on several real-world datasets across multiple forecasting horizons. The results demonstrate that the proposed method consistently improves forecasting accuracy compared with frequently used forecasting methods.

title: IEEE Bulgaria Section Jt. Chapter CAS04/SSC37 Hosts CASS Distinguished Lecturer Prof. Ricardo Reis [CASS Chapter Highlights]
authors: Valeri Mladenov
publish: IEEE Circuits and Systems Magazine
abstract: As part of the IEEE Circuits and Systems Society Distinguished Lecturer (DL) Program, Prof. Ricardo Reis visited Bulgaria in November 2025 and delivered two invited lectures hosted by the Bulgarian CASS Chapter and Bulgarian universities. Invited by Prof. Valeri Mladenov, Chair of the IEEE Bulgaria Section Joint Chapter CAS04/SSC37, the lectures addressed both emerging trends and advanced methodologies in microelectronics and physical design automation. The events attracted strong interest from the academic community and contributed to strengthening collaboration between the IEEE Circuits and Systems community and academic institutions in Bulgaria.

title: FedForward: Efficient Federated Learning with Back Propagation-Free Training and Privacy Guarantee_supp1-3681968.pdf
authors: Shengjie Zhao
publish: N/A
abstract: In Federated Learning (FL), stragglers refer to the slow devices with delayed updates that stall model aggregations at the server. Because of the inevitable system heterogeneity, low-end devices easily become stragglers, which are usually excluded from training for efficiency, despite the fact that they may possess valuable data for the training of the global model. This is especially devastating for application scenarios where devices' capacities vary drastically, such as Internet-of-Things (IoT). To overcome this challenge, we design FedForward, an FL architecture that replaces expensive backpropagation with forward gradients on low-end devices, so that they may update their local models based on the global model changes from the previous communication round. With FedForward and its variant FedForward-PM, training time on low-end devices can be greatly reduced, without sacrificing the performance of the global model. Our analysis and experiments show that FedForward is guaranteed to converge at the same speed as FedAvg, while significantly reducing the computation time (52.32% and higher, compared to FedAvg and other FL solutions) and computational resource consumption (up to 77.78%) for low-end devices, in scenarios under both IID and non-IID distributions. To combat inference attacks in FedForward-PM, a light-load privacy protection measure is designed for the server, by which data leakage can be minimized without sacrificing test accuracy. In a practical sense, FedForward and variants allow low-end devices to be included as much as possible in FL training, and are therefore especially meaningful for FL deployments in IoT environments.

title: Degradation-Adaptive Denoising: Aligning Diffusion Models with Physics of Video Snapshot Compressive Imaging
authors: Mingjin Zhang, Mingrui Li, Jie Guo, Yunsong Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
abstract: Video Snapshot Compressive Imaging (SCI) captures multiple video frames in a single exposure, enabling efficient reconstruction of high-speed scenes for motion analysis and event detection. Existing SCI in coded aperture compressive temporal imaging (CACTI) methods predominantly rely on feedforward deep networks with fixed denoising strategies. However, they lack alignment with the SCI physical inverse model and struggle to balance motion detail recovery and static background smoothing. In this paper, we propose PCD-Diffusion for Video SCI, the first diffusion-based reconstruction framework for Video SCI, which reformulates the inverse problem as a progressive denoising process. Specifically, we design a Physically-Constrained Dynamic Diffusion (PCD-Diffusion) model, introducing a region-adaptive diffusion schedule and spatiotemporal residual estimation. This method explicitly aligns the denoising process with SCI’s spatially non-uniform and temporally evolving residual distribution. Additionally, a motion prior-guided diffusion schedule and a Gauss-guided spatiotemporal adaptive residual estimation dynamically steer the denoising trajectory, ensuring accurate motion detail restoration and physically consistent reconstructions. Extensive results on simulated and real datasets verify the superior reconstruction fidelity and temporal coherence of the proposed PCD-Diffusion framework over existing approaches. Code will be released upon publication.

title: Adaptive Split Federated Learning with Behavioral Digital Twins and Multi-Head Temporal Networks for Internet of Vehicles Security
authors: Farhan Ullah, Khalid Mahmood, Umar Raza, Jawad Ahmad, Jenhui Chen, Rubal Jeet, Yue Zhao
