title: PDO-SFCM: Prediction-Driven Orchestration for SFC Migration in SAGIN via Fine-Tuned Large Time-Series Model and DRL
authors: Jiang Mo, Ke Zhao, Limei Peng, Hsiao-Chun Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: Space-air-ground integrated networks (SAGINs) have emerged as an appealing enabling technology for the next-generation ubiquitous connectivity. By extending terrestrial networks with aerial and space platforms, SAGIN can provide seamless coverage and flexible resource-access across various altitudes. However, dynamic link conditions, intermittent connectivity, and heterogeneous latency constraints would often introduce serious challenges to the service function chain (SFC) migration and orchestration. In this work, we introduce a novel PDO-SFCM (prediction-driven orchestration for SFC migration) approach, which utilizes a fine-tuned large time-series model (LTM) for network status prediction and a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) module for proactive SFC migration in SAGINs. In detail, the fine-tuned LTM predicts multi-horizon estimates of SFC arrivals and per virtual network function (per-VNF) resource demands, which will form the observation space of the DRL agent. The DRL module thus schedules appropriate migration actions on the cost-augmented time-expanded graph (C-eTEG), which can satisfy the feasibility subject to the bandwidth, buffering, and precedence constraints. Extensive simulation results demonstrate that our proposed new PDO-SFCM scheme consistently greatly improves the acceptance rate, reduces the end-to-end delay, and lowers the migration cost in comparison with DRL baselines under different prediction settings. Our proposed new scheme can significantly leverage the SAGIN performance by the devised foundation-level time-series prediction and learning-based orchestration mechanisms.

title: Magnetic Field Reconstruction and Internal Current Inversion of GIS via Physics-Informed Deep Operator Network
authors: Xiangyu Guan, Xiaokun Chen, Hang Zhang, Chaoqun Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: With multi-contact element parallel design, current distribution is closely related with assembly status of plug-in connector used in gas insulated switchgear (GIS). This work designs a physics-informed deep operator network (PI-DeepONet) framework to retrieve the internal current distribution among contact elements from reinforcement learning (RL) optimized sparse outside tank surface magnetic sensor array. The Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) based RL algorithm is firstly adopted to confirm optimal tank surface magnetic sensor layout. The PI-DeepONet consists of two parallel sub-networks, the branch network is employed to encode feature magnetic field and current vector and from magnetic sensor array observed data. Meanwhile, the trunk network to encodes space-time coordinates, efficiently mapping measurements to field solutions through operator learning. The governing partial differential equations for electromagnetic fields are embedded as physics loss terms to ensure physical consistency, while discrepancies between reconstructed and measured tank surface magnetic fields, along with total operational current differences, are incorporated as data loss terms to maintain measurement fidelity. Effectiveness of proposed method is compared with finite element analysis (FEA) and physical experiments, results show that the proposed approach achieves a magnetic field reconstruction maximum absolute error of 0.33 mT using 7 optimized outside magnetic sensors. With 28 times faster prediction speed compared to conventional simulations, while maintaining a current inversion maximum absolute error of 0.55 A under background noise conditions. Proposed technique can be implemented on magnetic-based monitor systems, thus enabling accurate internal contact fault diagnosis of GIS.

title: How Software Engineers Engage with AI: A Pragmatic Workflow
authors: Vəhid Gəruslu, Zafar Jafarov, Aytan Mövsümova, Atif Namazov
publish: IEEE Software
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools such as GitHub Copilot and ChatGPT are increasingly used in software engineering (SE) for tasks such as code, test, and documentation generation. However, engineers often face uncertainty about when to trust, refine, or discard AI-generated artifacts. We present a pragmatic workflow, complemented by a four-quadrant decision model, that formalizes how developers iteratively prompt, inspect, refine, and, when needed, fall back to manual work. The workflow and decision model were derived from a grey literature review and field observations across three industrial settings in Türkiye and Azerbaijan. Two realworld scenarios demonstrate the workflow’s practical value, showing how engineers navigate key decision points when using AI. Our approach offers lightweight, semi-formalization of the decision-making process engineers already follow – implicitly or explicitly – when engaging with AI tools in everyday SE tasks, supporting more deliberate and quality-aware use of AI.".

title: RAG-Augmented Explainable Early Warning System for Acute Kidney Injury in the ICU: A KDIGO-Aligned XGBoost Pipeline with SHAP-Driven Evidence Retrieval on MIMIC-IV
authors: Kerem Gencer
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Acute kidney injury (AKI) affects up to 50% of critically ill patients and is independently associated with increased in-hospital mortality. Early, accurate identification of at-risk patients is critical yet remains challenging due to the heterogeneous presentation and multifactorial etiology of AKI in the intensive care unit (ICU). This paper presents AKI-PREDICT, a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)-augmented clinical decision support framework that integrates gradient-boosted machine learning with SHAP-based explainability and biomedical evidence retrieval for early AKI prediction. The system is trained and evaluated on MIMIC-IV v3.1, a large real-world ICU database comprising 29,454 adult patients after KDIGO-aligned cohort construction. A strict temporal design confines all predictive features to the first 24 hours of ICU admission, with the AKI outcome window defined as hours 24–72, ensuring complete elimination of data leakage. The primary XGBoost model achieves an AUROC of 0.806 and AUPRC of 0.781 on the held-out test set, outperforming LightGBM (AUROC 0.804) and Random Forest (AUROC 0.786). A secondary multi-class XGBoost model performs KDIGO staging (Stage 0–3), achieving 68.4% overall accuracy. SHAP analysis identifies urine output rate (mL/kg/h, 0–24h), maximum serum creatinine, patient weight, and mechanical ventilation as the principal predictors of AKI onset. An ablation study demonstrates that urine output features contribute the largest single-group performance gain (ΔAUROC = 0.033), followed by laboratory values (ΔAUROC = 0.022). The RAG component retrieves semantically relevant excerpts from the KDIGO 2012 Clinical Practice Guideline and PubMed literature using SHAP-driven query construction and BioBERT-based embeddings indexed with FAISS, generating patient-specific, evidence-grounded clinical reports. Subgroup analysis confirms consistent discriminative performance across CKD, sepsis, vasopressor exposure, and age strata. AKI-PREDICT provides a fully reproducible, open-source pipeline linking prediction, explanation, and guideline-grounded evidence in a single workflow.

title: A 50–110 GHz UltraWideband Waveguide Diplexer Based on Novel Branch-Guide Coupler Utilizing Waveguide Cutoff Characteristics
authors: Yuchi Zhang, Bingli Dai, Yinian Feng, Zhongqian Niu, Yonghong Zhang, Bo Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article presents a low-loss, high-isolation diplexer based on a novel ultrawideband branch-guide coupler (BGC). To maximize the operating bandwidth, a low-frequency transmission zero (TZ) is introduced by exploiting the waveguide cutoff characteristic, without incurring additional spurious responses. Furthermore, a dual-ridge waveguide branch structure is proposed, which generates a high-frequency TZ while reducing manufacturing complexity. For verification, a 12-branch BGC and a waveguide diplexer operating from 50 to 110 GHz were designed and fabricated. Measured results are in good agreement with simulations. For the BGC, the measured amplitude imbalance is below 0.9 dB (0.4 dB on average), and the return loss and isolation are better than 16 dB. For the diplexer, the measured insertion loss is below 1.3 dB for the 50–75 GHz band and below 0.9 dB for the 75–110 GHz band, with an isolation of 24 dB achieved between the two contiguous channels.

title: A Reference-Free Framework for Stereoscopic Image Quality Evaluation Using Wavelet and Sharpness Features of Scene Components_supp1-3693751.pdf
authors: Pankaj Kumar Raghuwanshi
publish: N/A
abstract: Stereoscopic-3D (S3D) technology has gained widespread adoption recently across multimedia-based consumer applications due to its ability to develop immersive perceptual experiences. With this tremendous utilization, assessing the generated S3D content quality is essential for achieving consistent and reliable performance across diverse multimedia systems and devices. To automate this process, we develop a reference-free image quality assessment (IQA) algorithm based on performing a multiscale biorthogonal wavelet transform on the cohesive color map derived from an S3D image. We model the resulting sub-bands using a univariate generalized Gaussian distribution and estimate the corresponding fitting coefficients. We demonstrate that the computed features effectively distinguish distortions and estimate the Chi-square distance between test image features and pristine model parameters to measure the primitive chrominance feature of an S3D image. Further, we perform a second derivative Laplacian operator on the cohesive color map to measure the overall sharpness of chromatic information of an S3D scene. Next, we compute the perceptual image quality evaluator on the left and right scenes to estimate the overall luminance feature of an S3D image. Finally, chromatic and luminance-based features are linearly combined to calculate the overall quality score of an S3D image. We evaluate our model on four benchmark datasets (LIVE Phase I & II, Waterloo Phase I & II), demonstrating its superior performance against eighteen existing IQA models, including 2D and 3D opinion-unaware and aware algorithms. This algorithm provides a highly effective solution to ensure the data quality required for the next generation of multimedia applications. The source code for the proposed opinion-unaware model can be accessed via the following: Google Drive link.

title: Brain-Controlled Target Speech Extraction Based on Disentanglement and Progressive Dual-Modal Fusion
authors: Xilei Ling, Haixian Wang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: Target speech extraction in cocktail-party environments remains challenging, especially when acoustic mixtures are highly overlapped and conventional speaker cues are unreliable. Inspired by the staged neural mechanism of auditory attention, we propose DPFNet, a brain-controlled target speech extraction framework based on disentanglement and progressive dual-modal fusion. DPFNet decomposes electroencephalogram (EEG) auditory attention cues into a static component for stable target-related prior and a dynamic component for time-varying tracking. Built on a SepFormer-style backbone, the model introduces a static auditory attention modulation separator to inject stable EEG priors into dual-path speech separation through adaptive modulation, and a dynamic auditory attention adaptive calibration module to further refine continuous acoustic representations using cross-modal interaction and temporal gating. In this way, DPFNet progressively integrates static identity-related information and dynamic auditory attention cues, yielding a neurophysiologically informed framework for brain-guided target speech extraction.

title: From Pose to Verifiable Constraints: Robust Video Assessment of Early Post-THA Sit-to-Stand
authors: Sinan Chen, Shaojuan Huang, Xinyun Bai, Qiting Lin, Shuping Yu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: Early-stage rehabilitation after total hip arthroplasty (THA) increasingly relies on home/ward videos, yet pose-estimation noise and silent perturbations can yield over-confident motion-quality assessments. We propose a security-aware, constraint-optimized pipeline that converts a single RGB sit-to-stand (STS) clip into verifiable safety and interpretable quality outputs. A 2D pose estimator produces 17 keypoints per frame; reliability-aware gating and normalization enable robust temporal feature extraction (e.g., trunk angle, hip/knee angles, asymmetry, pelvis sway) and STS phase localization via vertical-velocity cues. Clinically grounded rules are formalized as hard constraints to generate a binary safety-validity indicator, while soft constraints are aggregated into a continuous quality score with penalty-based explanations. To address adversarial (or nuisance) keypoint-space perturbations, we evaluate robustness under controlled Gaussian noise and quantify score inflation and degradation; results reveal non-monotonic behavior that can inflate naive soft-constraint scores. We introduce reliability-aware regularization that couples penalty aggregation with a clip-level confidence ratio, mitigating inflation and stabilizing degradation trends. A pilot study on two post-operative day-3 THA patients recorded at Foshan Hospital of Traditional Chinese Medicine demonstrates clinically meaningful differentiation in asymmetry, trunk variability, and post-stand stability, while improving robustness against keypoint perturbations.

title: Blockchain-Enabled Secure Transaction Architecture for Fraud Detection and Risk Management in Financial Institutions
authors: Jacob Davis K, A. Thilagaraj
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: The Fraud protection and risk management are still major concerns for financial institutions in highly digitized settings. This paper presents a blockchain-based secure transaction system that utilizes HyperDimensional Computing (HDC) and blockchain technology to provide enhanced institutional robustness against fraudulent transactions. The blockchain technology enables the creation of immutable and transparent records that promote trust and accountability, while the HDC technology provides unparalleled capabilities in anomaly detection for complex high-dimensional financial data. The proposed system takes advantage of the best of both worlds and provides an excellent platform for real-time fraud detection and risk analysis. The experimental result shows an accuracy of 98%, thus confirming the efficacy of the proposed approach in achieving a balance between security, scalability, and efficiency. This research aims to help in the development of intelligent financial infrastructure using computational intelligence and trust in a decentralized manner, which will serve as a way forward for secure and trustworthy digital transactions. However, apart from the benefits of this research, the proposed architecture is also aimed at being deployable in real-world financial systems. By incorporating HyperDimensional Computing into the blockchain transaction process, the system is able to adapt to the latest patterns of fraud without having to extensively retrain. The decentralized system also ensures that the risk assessments are transparent and verifiable. More importantly, the system is able to adapt to the latest regulations on computational efficiency.

title: TcNc: Token comparison and noise correction for Weakly-Supervised Semantic Segmentation
authors: Weijie Su
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: Weakly supervised semantic segmentation methods utilizing image-level labels have garnered increasing attention due to their significant reduction in annotation costs and broader applicability. CNN-based weakly supervised semantic segmentation methods have limitations, as CNNs struggle to capture sufficient global information. While the emergence of Transformer networks effectively addresses this issue, Vision Transformer (VIT) networks often overlook the distinction between global and local image features. This leads to excessive smoothing and poor edge detail recognition in target regions. We propose the Token Comparison and Noise Correction (TcNc) weakly supervised semantic segmentation method. This approach employs a Token Comparison Module (TCM) to address VIT's over-smoothing issue and utilizes a Class Comparison Module (CCM) to encourage CAM activation across more object regions. Additionally, a Noise Correction Module (NCM) is designed to evaluate the quality of initial pseudo labels, then outputs a new pseudo label to correct weak labels into more reliable ones, improving pseudo label noise through an online approach. Experiments on the PASCAL VOC 2012 dataset demonstrate that the proposed TcNc significantly outperforms other single-stage and multi-stage methods.

title: NAVIGATE: Multi-Agent Graph-Based Vulnerability Detection with Adversarial Severity Assessment
authors: Yang Guo
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: Automated vulnerability severity assessment is critical for prioritizing security remediation efforts, yet existing approaches face significant challenges. Traditional text-based methods rely solely on vulnerability descriptions, lacking the technical depth present in source code. Meanwhile, Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown promise in code understanding but struggle with long input sequences and lack structured semantic information, leading to unreliable severity predictions. This paper presents NAVIGATE, a multi-agent graph-centric framework that synergizes Code Property Graphs (CPGs) with LLM-powered reasoning for automated vulnerability severity assessment. A key innovation is the deployment of an autonomous ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) agent with a documentation subagent for dynamic query generation, enabling the discovery of complex vulnerability patterns without relying on predefined static rules. Another core contribution is the adversarial multi-agent debate mechanism using AG2 (AutoGen 2), where Security Analyst, Red Team Expert, Blue Team Expert, and Severity Judge agents engage in structured deliberation to achieve consensus-based severity ratings. Our approach employs a multi-stage pipeline comprising: (1) CPG-based code representation, (2) dual-agent vulnerability discovery with context compression, (3) dataflow-guided slicing with Tree-sitter-based syntactic reconstruction, (4) LLM-based path filtering with Top-K selection, and (5) adversarial debate for CVSS v3-aligned severity classification. Experiments on a benchmark of 594 CVEs in C/C++ demonstrate that NAVIGATE achieves a severity classification accuracy of 72.73%, F1-score of 70.0%, and MCC of 0.544. Compared to the SVACL baseline [1] (63.47% accuracy), our dataflow-guided slicing approach—which uses Joern’s inter-procedural reachableByFlows analysis to extract vulnerability-relevant code paths—improves accuracy by 9.26 percentage points.

title: Residual Deformable Attention Framework with Detection Transformer for Early and Accurate Skin Cancer Detection from Dermoscopic Images
authors: Karamala Naveen, M. Humera Khanam
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Melanoma is an exceptionally prevalent and deadly type of skin cancer that affects people all over the world. Early detection significantly increases melanoma patients' chances of recovery. Deep learning techniques are currently becoming more and more popular for helping in melanoma early detection. Even if image classifiers perform well, depending only on them damages the application's credibility and makes it hard to suggest the reason behind the predictions. To address this issue, this study proposes an advanced deep structured learning-based model for accurate skin cancer detection. In the initial phase, the input images are gathered from benchmark databases. These gathered images undergo an image pre-processing phase where Detection Transformer and Bilateral Filtering are applied to reduce noise while preserving important edges of the skin lesion. The pre-processed images are leveraged by the Residual Deformable Detection Transformer Network (RD-DeTNet) for the detection of skin cancer. Finally, the proposed RD-DeTNet offers the skin cancer detection outcome. The performance of the proposed system is verified using various performance metrics, demonstrating its superiority compared to traditional detection models. Therefore, it substantially enhanced clinical decision support and overall performance in computerized skin cancer analysis by enabling the early detection of malignant patterns and increasing diagnostic consistency.

title: Priority Aware Q-Learning for Multi Objective Task Scheduling in Cloud Computing
authors: Harini Kota, S Sudheer Mangalampalli, Pillareddy Vamsheedhar Reddy
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: In cloud computing, effective task scheduling is essential for enhancing system performance and reduce operating costs when workloads are dynamic. Our research aim is to improve the task-to-VM allocation choices by proposing a Priority-Aware Q-Learning based scheduling that includes task priority into the reinforcement learning. The proposed work uses a action-selection strategy to guarantee the stable and consistent scheduling behavior, in contrast to traditional stochastic reinforcement learning techniques. The extensive simulations are performed in SimPy using Google Cloud Jobs (GoCJ) to evaluate the model. When compared to the ACO and Q-Learning algorithms, experimental results show the improvement on proposed algorithm for makespan with 42.78% and 18.14%, Average waiting time with 36.88% and 16.42%, Average response time with 41.9% and 33.71% and cost with 33.87% and 17.18%. These improvements demonstrate that priority integration and effective workload distribution reduce delays as well as operational costs.

title: An Integrated Machine Learning Model for Accurate Stroke Diagnosis using CT Neuro Images
authors: B.Rama Subbaiah, Chenna Rajya Lakshmi, Kanike Lekha, Kummari Lavanya Kumari, Malipeddi Sai Tharun
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Stroke is one of the leading causes of death and disability in the world today and there is need to have quick and effective diagnostic methods. Computed Tomography (CT) is the preferred imaging modality in the assessment of acute stroke due to its availability and rapidity as well as the ability to distinguish ischemic and hemorrhagic lesions. Nevertheless, the manual interpretation process is time-intensive and prone to the variability, especially in the early subtle ischemic changes. This paper hypothesizes a multimodal machine learning platform that could incorporate neuroimaging, clinical and biochemical data in order to better detect and predict stroke. The process of extracting CT features is automated by the hybrid image encoder based on the EfficientNet and Vision Transformer (ViT) model, which allows capturing the details of the radiograph locally and the context on a large scale. Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and TabTransformer models are used to model structured electronic health record (EHR) data in order to detect complex clinical relationships. Also bio-indicators of stroke risk and severity are provided with ferroptosis-and inflammation-related biomarkers as additional biological data. To produce a unified diagnostic output cross-modal attention based fusion layer carries out the integration of all learnt representations. It is found that experimental outcomes include increased accuracy, less dependence on the manual, and better stroke prediction based on personalization as opposed to the single-modality baselines. This method will facilitate quicker and more accurate stroke evaluation using simultaneous and clinically significant multimodal data, which can have significant advantages in emergency care and early intervention.

title: Federated Transformer–CNN Hybrid Framework for Privacy-Aware Multi-Hospital Disease Risk Prediction
authors: Sanjay Gandhi Gundabatini, B Durga Sravani, Y.Sherly Priscilla, Bunga Revathi, D Ratnagiri, Madhava Rao Maganti
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: The digitization of multi-hospital health information has facilitated predictive disease risk modeling while also presenting problems about patient privacy, regulatory compliance, and limited cross-institutional data exchange. Traditional centralized learning approaches necessitate the transmission of sensitive patient data to distant servers, which is sometimes unfeasible in actual clinical settings. This article presents a privacy-preserving federated hybrid learning framework that combines Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) with Transformer-based contextual modeling for illness risk prediction in dispersed hospitals. The proposed architecture employs CNN encoders to extract spatial characteristics from medical pictures and structured patient records, whereas Transformer layers identify long-range correlations among temporal signals and demographic variables. Each participating hospital locally trains the model, sharing only encrypted model updates with a central coordinator for aggregation, thereby ensuring that raw patient data remains inside institutional confines. Experimental results indicate exceptional performance, attaining 96% accuracy, 93% precision, 95% recall, 94% F1-score, and 0.97 AUC, surpassing CNN-only, Transformer-only, and traditional hybrid federated models. The results underscore the efficacy of the proposed architecture as a scalable, privacy-conscious clinical decision-support system for collaborative multi-institutional disease risk assessment.

title: Enhancing Network Security with Software-Defined Networking
authors: J.Jenifer, Anita Antwiwaa, Sogy K Idicula, Gayathiri Devi S, Gomathi N.
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: With enterprises growing rapidly through new cloud technologies, they are exposed to new sophisticated cyber threats that traditional security solutions are not able to manage effectively. In addition to insufficient management capabilities, existing, traditional distributed architectures historically suffer from limited real-time, centralized visibility; inflexible controls; and slow policy implementation processes that create challenges to defending against modern networks. The paper presents an innovative, intelligent Software Defined Networking (SDN)-enabled security framework, which integrates centralized programmability, machine learning-based intrusion detection and automated mitigation to facilitate modern cyber defense. This architecture incorporates the division of control and data into separate planes, provides a global view of the network, enables the ability to dynamically manage traffic flows within the network, and allows consistent enforcement of security policies across multiple systems in the network, enabling the ability to secure today’s operating environments. The framework uses a customized hybrid approach to detect and identify both known and unknown threats; that is, the framework uses both signature-based and supervised learning-based filtering. To validate the architecture, it was tested with standard benchmark intrusion data sets in realistic laboratory conditions, while the performance evaluation of the architecture shows that it is highly successful in detecting potential indicator of attacks, providing a good level of precision and recall, producing a low false positive rate, requiring a minimal amount of additional resources to implement automated responses to network traffic, while minimizing the network overhead of automated responses. Therefore, the results support the conclusion that combining an intelligent analytical capability with SDN can meet the cybersecurity requirements of large enterprise environments and networks through a dependable scalable, adaptable and efficient solution.

title: Real-Time Navigation Assistant for Visually Impaired People using YOLOv8 and Voice Alerts
authors: K.Parvathi, Thota Kesava Rao, P Srinivasa Rao, S. Gopinath
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: The lack of situational awareness and slow perception of the obstacles around leads to safe navigation of visually impaired people in dynamic outdoor and indoor environments as a primary challenge. Despite the recent assistive navigation systems that use computer vision and audio feedback, most of the current methods have high computational latency, low detection accuracy, and low real-time functioning in complex settings. To overcome these weaknesses, the current research paper suggests a real-time navigation helper, which combines visual perception with deep learning and adaptive voice navigation. This proposed system provides a framework called the YOLOv8 object detection that is used to detect and localize the obstacles that are of interest to navigation like pedestrians, vehicles, exits, stairsteps, and street fixtures using live camera streams. Items found are compared in terms of spatial relevance and distance to the navigation route of the user, and context-based voice notifications are created to give appropriate direction. The system is lightweight and can be deployed on edge devices, allowing the system to act as a continuous operating system that consumes little computational load. Benchmark dataset and real-time camera stream experimental analysis reveal high detection ability with a mean average precision (mAP) of 0.5) of 88.6, precision of 90.2, and recall of 86.9. The system has an average of 52 frames per second and an end-to-end of nearly 19 ms. All these findings suggest that the presented framework can be successfully used to increase the awareness of obstacles and enhance independent mobility and the safety of the visually impaired users.

title: Edge-Aware Unified Retinex Architecture for Adaptive Low-Light Enhancement of Images and Videos Across Diverse Domains in Real-Time
authors: Lakkakula Sai Raveena Sowgandhika, Madem Sanjana Sarvani, A. Yovan Felix
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: The images and videos which are captured in low-light conditions or dark environments will lead to poor visibility, quality, low contrast, amplified noise in the applications namely Surveillance, Autonomous Vehicles, Medical Imaging, Mobile Photography. The existing systems either fails in training the paired datasets, or the temporal consistency of videos. This work presents a real-time hybrid unified architecture that combines the classical image processing techniques and advanced deep learning model i.e., Multi-Scale Retinex (MSR) for enhancing the uneven lighting conditions, Multi-Scale Retinex Color Restoration for restoring the color (MSRCR), Zero-Reference Deep Curve Estimation (Zero-DCE++) for unsupervised adaptive refinement requiring no paired training data. The noise suppression stage which reduces the unwanted noise while preserving the fine structure details and boundaries. For video inputs, it undergoes temporal consistency which avoids the flickering between the frames and gains 30 frames per second. Performance evaluation across various applications yields Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) of 31.0db and Structural Similarity Index of Measure (SSIM) of 0.93, which confirms the effectiveness of the system. The lightweight architecture have the ability to enhance the images and videos while preserving the natural colors across various domains in real-world deployable environments.

title: Application and Evaluation of a Multi-Model Assisted Human-Review Workflow for Disaster Image Annotation
authors: Renjie Cao, Dezhi Wang, Wei Li, Hang Zheng, Jun Zhang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Information Security (GAIIS)
abstract: Disaster image annotation is time-consuming and demands considerable domain expertise from annotators. This paper presents a multi-model assisted human-review workflow for disaster image annotation. The workflow comprises three stages: dual-model independent annotation, consistency checking, and human review. Two multimodal vision-language models, Qwen3.5-Plus and GLM-4.6V, are invoked via API to independently perform bounding-box annotation and semantic description on the same image. The annotation results from the two models are then subjected to consistency checking by task type (IoU comparison for bounding boxes; DeepSeek-V3.2 comparison for semantic descriptions), and all results are presented to annotators as review references. Annotators may modify any annotation content, and the final results are submitted only after human confirmation. Using 600 wildfire images and 600 earthquake disaster images as experimental data, a controlled experiment was conducted comparing purely manual annotation (main-experiment Group A) with the proposed workflow (main-experiment Group B). Results show that the proposed workflow reduced annotation time by 72% in the wildfire scenario and 65% in the earthquake scenario compared to the purely manual group, while annotation accuracy differences between the two groups remained within 4 percentage points. This paper also identifies two typical scenarios where model assistance is limited.

title: Quantum Gantt Charts for Spatial Data Engineering: Visualizing Hyperslab Bottlenecks, Pipeline Multiplication, and Quantum Candidate Stages
authors: Haque Munshi Mahidul
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Distributed Computing and High Performance Computing (DCHPC)
abstract: Spatial data engineering pipelines routinely access multi-dimensional Earth-observation arrays by slicing regions-of-interest, time windows, and band subsets. In widely used array APIs, such subset access is expressed as a hyperslab selection. While hyperslabs are essential for partial access, they can also induce hyperslabbing overhead: repeated small subset reads that trigger disproportionate I/O, decompression, and orchestration costs relative to the numerical operations on the retrieved pixels. This paper develops a standards-grounded, visualization-first narrative based on Quantum Gantt Charts (QGantt) and Quantum Network Diagrams (QND). The manuscript contributes a compact diagram grammar tailored to spatial indexing, chunked storage, masks, and joins; three synthetic case studies with explicit numbers showing how hyperslabbing arises; a contrast between QGantt and BPMN/UML; a roadmap for a JavaScript prototype that renders QGantt from JSON into interactive diagrams; and a clear novelty statement contrasting QGantt with existing GIS workflow representations and quantum-inspired remote sensing work. A scope disclaimer prevents over-claims and keeps quantum references as annotations only.

title: A Novel Deep Learning Approaches for Brain Stroke Detection System on CT Images
authors: Anitha Thangasamy, Raghi K R, Kommi Bharath, Kondragunta Bobby
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Electronic Devices and Intelligent Systems (ICSEDIS)
abstract: Brain stroke has been a leading cause of death and irreversible neurological disabilities in the world, and thus the diagnosis of neurological disorders in the early stages and correctly will ensure successful clinical management. The proposed algorithmic system of stroke-impacted region identification and segmentation in the CT scan of the brain incorporates the deep learning and machine learning approaches. The pretrained convolutional neural network models (ResNet18 and EfficientNetB0) were used to determine whether the CT scans were stroke or non-stroke. The classical machine learning classifiers and novel deep learning approaches are used to further improve the diagnostic. The experiment results show that the hemorrhagic stroke is accurately determined. The proposed deep learning models with traditional machine learning methods to help clinicians to brain stroke earlier.

title: Analyzing Notebook Histories to Understand Data Visualization Workflows
authors: David Koop, Colin Brown, Hamed Alhoori, Maoyuan Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Visualization design is often a demanding process that involves trying different encodings, exploring different data transformations, and refining details. While there have been important studies of these workflows, the lower-level, code-intensive practices remain underexplored. Exploratory notebook tools have allowed designers to rapidly iterate on visualizations. We use publicly-available version histories of notebooks to study how users work in these environments, observing both how they build new visualizations from existing templates or previous work and how they refine visualizations over time. We examine the interplay between data manipulation and visualization, and classify the types of changes made when updating visual encodings. We also analyze the impact of different frameworks by comparing two code-oriented libraries and a chart wizard. Finally, we examine how interactions with notebooks have changed over the years, including after the widespread availability of AI. These analyses help us understand how users iterate to produce visualizations over time using different frameworks.

title: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications Publication Information
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications

title: Instruction-Guided Distribution Maximization for General Few-Shot Intent Recognition_supp1-3692528.pdf
authors: YaJun Du
publish: N/A
abstract: Intent recognition captures the deep needs of users from natural language, and few-shot intent recognition addresses data scarcity. Existing methods treat single- and multi-label intent recognition as separate classification tasks, and discriminative models typically require fine-tuning when generalizing to new intents, which limits generality. To this end, in this study, we re-think the single- and multi-label intent recognition task as a unified natural language generation task rather than a discriminative classification problem. After instruction pre-training on a general intent dataset, the language model (LM) directly generalizes to few-shot target data without fine-tuning. However, limited data and large distribution shifts severely challenge generalization. Therefore, in this paper, we propose an Instruction-Guided Distribution Maximization (IGDM) method to improve the robustness of the model and enhance generalization. IGDM prompts the model to learn a broader decision boundary by enlarging the maximum error of LM between the user utterance and the corresponding intent. Formally, we perform a two-level optimization strategy to obtain the inner instruction-guided sample distribution maximization and the outer model error minimization, respectively. The inner optimization objective is the sample distribution maximization, which is achieved by performing gradient ascent according to LM in instruction learning. The outer optimization objective is the LM, whose optimization is implemented by gradient descent in instruction learning combined with samples from the distribution maximized according to the LM. Extensive theoretical and inference proofs demonstrate the superior robustness and generalization capabilities of IGDM. To verify this, we conduct comprehensive experiments across 20 single-label and 7 multi-label widely used intent recognition benchmarks. The results demonstrate that IGDM achieves superior performance over existing methods across all benchmarks.

title: PEAK: PUF-Based End-to-End Authentication and Key-Exchange for IoT
authors: Chandranshu Gupta, Gaurav Varshney, Gaurav Kumar, Satyadev Ahlawat
publish: IEEE Embedded Systems Letters
abstract: We propose a lightweight end-to-end authentication and key-exchange protocol for resource-constrained IoT devices that avoids computationally expensive cryptographic operations by leveraging Physically Unclonable Functions (PUFs) and employs a lightweight Fuzzy Extractor (FE) to reliably handle PUF noise. The protocol relies exclusively on lightweight hash, XOR, and symmetric encryption/decryption operations. Moreover, the proposed protocol mitigates the storage overhead by avoiding the maintenance of large CRP repositories at the server. Security properties are formally verified using the scyther tool. A prototype implementation using two ESP32-based IoT nodes and a server further demonstrates its practicality for real-world deployments. Finally, benchmarking against state-of-the-art schemes indicates that the proposed protocol incurs comparatively low computation time and communication cost, highlighting its efficiency and lightweight nature.

title: Regulation of Multiharmonic Components for Nine-Phase Open-End Winding Permanent Magnet Synchronous Machines
authors: Haifeng Wang, Zhiyuan Zhang, Xibo Yuan, Xinzhen Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: The nine-phase open-end winding (OW) permanent magnet synchronous machine (PMSM) integrates the advantages of multiphase and OW topologies, making it an alternative solution for high-power propulsions. However, there is still a lack of efficient modulation methods for multiple (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bm{\geq}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>3) decoupling subspaces to address the multiharmonic current issue in this system. In this article, the advantages of model predictive control in handling multiobjectives and the virtual voltage vector (VVV) technique are utilized to achieve the active regulation of the fundamental, third-, fifth-, and seventh-order components. Unlike existing harmonic suppression strategies in multiphase AC machine drives, this study designs four sets of decoupled VVV to achieve the independent predictive control in four subspaces. Moreover, a multisubspace weight optimization algorithm is proposed to address the loss of harmonic control capability under voltage constraints. The proposed strategy is simple and easy to implement on industrial control platforms. Finally, experimental results validated the effectiveness of the proposed strategy, including a cost-performance comparison of controlling various harmonic components and selecting different constraint modes.

title: A 6G Driven Multiclass Power Efficient Dynamic Bandwidth Allocation (MPE-DBA) Scheme for Passive Optical Network (PON)
authors: Elie Inaty, Charbel Maroun, Ghattas Akkad, Ali Mansour, Martin Maier
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: The latest ITU IMT-2030 recommendations for sixth generation (6G) networks have imposed strict specifications on communication systems. Some of these requirements include increased throughput, ultra-low delay and jitter, differentiated services, and energy efficiency. Current dynamic bandwidth allocation (DBA) schemes for passive optical networks (PON) may meet some of these requirements, yet they fail to fulfill other recommendations, especially energy efficiency. Therefore, we propose a new PON architecture, whose objective is to offer flexibility in meeting the IMT-2030 recommendations. It uses a multiclass power efficient dynamic bandwidth allocation (MPE-DBA) scheme that helps achieving both differentiated services and sustainability in terms of energy and cost. For computational efficiency, we propose a two stages Mamdani fuzzy inference system (FIS). The inputs of the first FIS are the latency and cost of the 6G traffic, whereas the latency and cost of the non-6G (N6G) traffic are the inputs of the second FIS stage. Both FISs use the variation in the number of channels as output. The proposed algorithm achieves less than 100 μs delay, less than 10μs jitter and high aggregate throughput for the 6G packets. In addition, it reduces the power consumption by three times and the cost of traffic transmission by four times as compared to the state-of-the art solution.

title: IEEE Brain Hosts 2025 IEEE Brain Discovery and Neurotechnology Workshop [Society News]
authors: Cynthia Weber, Vesna Sossi, Ljiljana Trajkovic
publish: IEEE Circuits and Systems Magazine
abstract: Advances in understanding of the brain in health and disease is leading to groundbreaking discoveries and engineering solutions. Development and deployment of effective neurotechnology and means of studying the brain through neuroimaging techniques and machine learning requires an integrated approach as well as close collaboration among the neuroengineering community, neuroscientists, and clinical practitioners. The 2025 IEEE Brain Discovery and Neurotechnology Workshop brought together researchers and practitioners across academia, industry, and the clinical profession, highlighted innovative neurotechnology and brain research methods and fostered opportunities for cross-disciplinary collaboration.

title: Machine Learning Enabled Movement Pattern Recognition and Monitoring Using a Pomegranate Membrane Based Sensor_supp1-3692686.docx
authors: Harminder Singh
publish: N/A
abstract: Wearable Intelligent activity recognition has emerged as a rapidly growing research area with significant relevance to ubiquitous computing and intelligent human–computer interaction, enabling applications in healthcare, fitness assessment, gaming, tactical military systems, and indoor navigation. Wearable sensing technologies, particularly triboelectric nanogenerator (TENG)–based sensors, have gained considerable attention due to their self-powered operation, mechanical flexibility, stability, and cost-effectiveness. In this work, a biodegradable and sustainable triboelectric nanogenerator sensor is designed and developed to promote environmentally friendly electronic systems. The proposed device utilizes a pomegranate membrane as the primary triboelectric material, resulting in a biodegradable pomegranate membrane–based TENG (PM-TENG). The PM-TENG demonstrates a maximum output voltage of 48.3 V and is capable of charging multiple commercial capacitors. Furthermore, the sensor effectively captures, monitors, and distinguishes human activities, including walking, running, and jumping. To enhance activity recognition performance, a machine learning–based framework is introduced for efficient signal processing and accurate classification of human activities. The integration of machine learning models with the PM-TENG sensor shows superior performance, with the histogram gradient boosting (HGB) model achieving the highest classification accuracy of 97.78%. These results highlight the potential of the proposed PM-TENG sensor as a sustainable, self-powered, and intelligent wearable platform for high-precision human activity monitoring.

title: Ultra-Wideband, Polarization-Insensitive, and Angular-Stable Microwave Absorber Based on ITO-Deposited FSS
authors: Awanish Kumar
publish: IEEE Letters on Electromagnetic Compatibility Practice and Applications
abstract: This work presents a polarization insensitive, highly angular stable, and ultrawideband microwave absorber operating over 2.1-25 GHz. The proposed absorber uses a periodic array of vertically arranged lossy frequency selective surface (FSS) patterns. The presence of losses inside the resonators with multiple resonant modes enables a wide absorption band. Moreover, the symmetry within the FSS unit cells ensures the polarization insensitivity. The subwavelength periodicity of the unit cell, along with vertically stacked FSS layers, contributes to high angular stability. Based on simulation analysis, the proposed absorber achieves more than 90% absorption over 2.1–25 GHz for incidence angles up to 60°, while maintaining an ultra-thin thickness of 0.078 λL and a fractional bandwidth of 169%, offering an improved trade-off among bandwidth, thickness, and angular stability compared to existing FSS-based absorbers. Furthermore, the absorber is fabricated with three Indium Tin Oxide (ITO)-engraved FSS layers, foam, and FR-4 substrates and characterized in an anechoic chamber. The experimental results verify the absorber’s high performance. Theoretical and experimental investigations confirm that the proposed design holds strong potential for radar cross-section (RCS) mitigation and electromagnetic interference suppression.

title: A Secure Machine Learning-Based Framework for Aggressive Driving Behavior Analysis in IoV System
authors: SAKSHAM MITTAL, MOHAMMAD WAZID, ASHOK KUMAR DAS, DEVESH PRATAP SINGH, VIVEKANANDA BHAT K, Sunil Prajapat
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Vehicular Technology
abstract: The increase in the number of road accidents is one of the major global concerns nowadays. The aggressive and abnormal driving behavior of the drivers is the primary cause of this issue. Such behavior can be observed frequently in urban traffic environments, leading to severe consequences including injuries, fatalities, and property damage. Therefore, it is very important to have a certain intelligent mechanism that can monitor driving behavior in real time and take appropriate action if it is found to be aggressive or abnormal. This can be possible by integrating the Internet of Things (IoT), digital twins, and machine learning technology. In this paper, we have proposed a secure machine learning-based framework for the detection of aggressive driving behavior in Internet of Vehicles (IoV) systems. It is assumed that smart IoT computing devices, such as sensors and cameras, are installed on roads and in moving vehicles to capture data such as sudden acceleration, harsh braking, sharp turns, tailgating, etc. This data is sent to the cloud servers in a secure manner, incorporating mutual authentication and key establishment between communicating entities. Machine learning models that are deployed on cloud servers are trained and tested against such data to analyze driving patterns. The machine learning classifiers that are utilized in the proposed work are Random Forest, K-nearest neighbor, AdaBoost, and Multi-layer perceptron (MLP) classifier, where Random Forest has achieved the maximum accuracy and F1-score. Finally, the proposed scheme is also compared with other existing schemes based on different parameters and outperforms those schemes.

title: Principled Uncertainty Decomposition with Bayesian Ensemble Transformers for Trustworthy Intrusion Detection_supp1-3694131.pdf
authors: Xiufeng Liu
publish: N/A
abstract: Network intrusion detection systems (IDS) are critical for cybersecurity, yet existing approaches often provide overconfident predictions without reliable uncertainty estimates, hindering critical decision-making. We introduce a novel Bayesian ensemble transformer framework for uncertainty-aware intrusion detection that delivers both strong detection performance and well-calibrated confidence. Our framework uniquely combines transformer architectures with ensemble methods to achieve a principled decomposition of prediction uncertainty into epistemic (model uncertainty) and aleatoric (data uncertainty) components, offering actionable insights for security analysts. We establish theoretical convergence guarantees for this approach under local convexity assumptions, supported by empirical validation. Comprehensive experiments across four benchmark datasets (NSL-KDD, CICIDS2017, UNSW-NB15, and SWaT) demonstrate F1-scores ranging from 77.55% to 97.00%, with particularly strong performance on UNSW-NB15 (97.00%), alongside excellent calibration with Expected Calibration Error (ECE) ranging from 0.0248 to 0.2278. Adversarial robustness analysis shows minimal performance degradation under sophisticated C&W (3.6% drop) and PGD attacks (8.0% drop), highlighting the framework's resilience. This work advances intrusion detection by providing interpretable uncertainty and robust performance, addressing a critical gap in current cybersecurity systems to enable more informed and reliable security decisions.

title: Hourly Precipitation Retrieval Using Fourier Neural Operator with GOES-16 and GOES-19 Inputs During the 4 July 2025 Texas Flash Flood_supp1-3693593.png
authors: Max Johncox
publish: N/A

title: Safe Optimal Formation of Continuous-Time Multiagent Systems via Maximum Entropy Adaptive Dynamic Programming
authors: Lulu Zhang, Huaguang Zhang, Tianbiao Wang, Jiawei Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: This article addresses the problem of formation control for multiagent systems operating in partially unknown dynamic environments. A safe optimal control method is proposed by employing maximum entropy adaptive dynamic programming (MEADP) in continuous-time systems, alongside high-order control barrier functions (HoCBFs). First, an MEADP algorithm for continuous-time systems is developed to encourage exploration and prevent convergence to suboptimal solutions under partially unknown system dynamics, with a comprehensive convergence proof of the algorithm provided. Then, safety constraints are formulated using HoCBFs, which are capable of handling safety constraints with higher relative degrees. These constraints are incorporated via quadratic programming to modify the maximum entropy optimal control policy, ensuring that the system states remain within a predefined safe set and effectively avoid collisions with obstacles. Moreover, the MEADP method utilizes a critic neural network to approximate the entropy-regularized Hamilton–Jacobi–Bellman equation, leading to a maximum entropy optimal control policy. Finally, simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach, making it suitable for safe formation control tasks in complex dynamic environments.

title: Multimodal Patient-Specific Identification of Atrial Flutter Circuits from ECG Time Series Using Explainable Machine Learning
authors: Samuel Ruipérez-Campillo, David Hernando, Elisa Ramírez, Sergio Castrejón, Cecilia Zapata, Carlos Rodríguez Carneiro, Julia E. Vogt, José Luis Merino, Francisco Castells, José Millet
publish: IEEE Journal of Translational Engineering in Health and Medicine
abstract: Objective: Accurate pre-procedural identification of atrial flutter (AFL) mechanisms can streamline mapping and indirectly inform ablation strategy, yet surface-electrocardiogram (ECG) criteria remain unreliable and circuit definition is typically confirmed invasively. Methods and procedures: We analyzed 97 consecutive patients undergoing electrophysiological (EP) study with simultaneous 12-lead ECG and EP-verified AFL subtype; adenosine-induced atrioventricular AV block enabled extraction of clean atrial segments. We reconstructed atrial vectorcardiograms (VCGs) and engineered interpretable descriptors of loop morphology and kinematics, including archetype cosine correlation, geometric complexity, and velocity-based slow-occupancy indices, then fused these with clinical variables in an explainable tree-ensemble model evaluated with nested cross-validation. Results: VCG loops exhibited subtype-specific archetypes (within-class correlation: 0.832±0.129 C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CCW</sub>, 0.874±0.154 C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CW</sub>, 0.647±0.127 PM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CCW</sub>, 0.667±0.159 PM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CW</sub>; C: common; PM: perimitral; CW: clockwise; CCW: counter-CW). On the test set, the multimodal Random-Forest improved discrimination over VCG-only and clinical-only baselines, achieving AUROC of 0.870 (C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CCW</sub>), 0.900 (C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CW</sub>), 0.840 (PM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CCW</sub>), and 0.790 (PM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CW</sub>), with high sensitivity for common AFL (0.833 and 0.929) and very high specificity for PM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CW</sub> (0.988). Conclusion: This interpretable framework provides a practical route to non-invasive, mechanism-oriented AFL stratification to support targeted mapping and more efficient ablation planning. Future work will focus on multicentre prospective validation and robust atrial-signal extraction without adenosine to broaden routine applicability. Clinical and Translational Impact Statement: Clinical Research. An interpretable ECG+clinical model predicts AFL mechanism pre-procedure, guiding mapping and ablation strategy. Integration: export 12-lead ECG, compute VCG features, and display subtype probability with key drivers in the EP lab.

title: Video-Centered Multimodal Learning for Non-Invasive Detection of Emotional Workload in Emotional Labor Settings
authors: Yejin Choi, Yejin Jang, Eunji Park
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Workers in emotional labor environments often followdisplay rules that require them to express emotions as directed by their boss or the company, regardless of their true feelings. These workers suppress their true feelings, which leads to ongoing mental issues and an accumulation of emotional workload. For example, in a call center situation, if a customer is shouting and making accusatory comments at the worker, the worker should respond in a positive way to the customer, even if they have negative feelings. However, it still remains difficult issue to estimate emotional workload in real-world settings using non-invasive methods. In this study, we propose a machine learning model that automatically detects workers’ emotional workload in emotional labor situations using multimodal data, including video and physiological data. We used a dataset designed based on a call center scenario and estimated emotional workload using two label: an objective label based on experimental conditions, and a subjective label based on workers’ self-reports. As a result, we found that geometric features derived from facial expressions captured in videos significantly contributed to model performance and that applying MultipleKernel Learning (MKL) improved classification accuracy, especially when detecting subjective emotional workload. This study demonstrates the potential of image-based and MKL-based models for scalable and non-invasive detection of emotional workload, suggesting that they can be effectively applied in real-world work settings.

title: U-Sleep-RBD-KAN: Joint Sleep Staging and REM Behavior Disorder Detection with Interpretable Nonlinear Learning
authors: Ghadeer Yousef, S. Metilda Florence, Ali Mahmoud Mayya
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Rapid Eye Movement Behavior Disorder (RBD) is a clinically significant sleep disorder that is often associated with neurodegenerative conditions, making early and reliable detection important in sleep medicine. Similarly, accurate sleep stage classification is essential for understanding sleep architecture and supporting clinical decision-making. However, manual scoring of polysomnography (PSG) recordings is time-consuming and subject to inter-scorer variability, which limits scalability and consistency in practical applications. This paper proposes a unified deep learning framework, U-Sleep-RBD-KAN, for simultaneous sleep stage classification and RBD detection using multimodal PSG signals. The model integrates a U-shaped fully convolutional temporal network for hierarchical feature extraction, a REM-aware attention mechanism to emphasize physiologically relevant REM-related segments, and a Kolmogorov–Arnold Network (KAN) layer for structured nonlinear prediction. The framework is trained end-to-end on the Sleep-EDF Expanded dataset from PhysioNet, using EEG, EOG, and EMG signals segmented into 30-second epochs without manual feature engineering. Experimental results show that the proposed method achieves a sleep stage classification accuracy of 96.4% with a macro F1-score of 0.95, while subject-level RBD detection achieves an accuracy of 97.1%, a macro F1-score of 0.97, and an AUC of 0.992. These results indicate consistent performance across both tasks under a unified evaluation setup. Overall, the proposed framework demonstrates the effectiveness of combining multi-scale temporal modeling, REM-focused attention, and structured nonlinear learning for automated sleep analysis.

title: Deep Learning based Automated Detection of Bacterial Keratitis from Corneal Images
authors: Sathishkumar M, Abitha A, Jeyiin Keerthani C, Abishai M, Arun Kumar T, Kiruthikha M
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: Bacterial keratitis is the serious corneal infection that rapidly leads to vision impairment or permanent blindness if the disease is not diagnosed at its early stage. Thus, the accurate detection of disease from the corneal images is challenging due to variations in lesion appearance, illumination conditions and image noise present in clinical images. To address the challenges, this research proposed the automated bacterial keratitis detection model using ConvNeXt–Efficient Transformer Network (CETNet) with the optimization strategy for improved learning performance. Initially, corneal images obtained from the publicly available dataset are preprocessed using Gaussian filter to remove noise and enhance structural features. The enhanced images are then fed into the CETNet architecture, wherein the feed-forward network (FFN) layer is enhanced using the hybrid ConvNeXt-EfficientNet module for obtaining improved spatial and hierarchical feature extraction. Besides, the loss function is optimized using the proposed Chaotic Beaver Behavior Optimization (C2BO) algorithm, which integrates chaotic Chebyshev mapping within the exploration phase of the Beaver Behavior Optimization algorithm to improve convergence and avoid local minima. The experimental analysis of the proposed model demonstrated the improved classification accuracy and robustness for automated bacterial keratitis detection.

title: 3D UAV Path Planning and Sensitivity Analysis Based on Artificial Life Algorithm
authors: Luda Zhen
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Smart Energy and Control Engineering (SECE)
abstract: D path planning is the core technical link to realize unmanned and autonomous operation, and the traditional unmanned and path planning algorithms are easy to fall into the problem of local optimization and slow convergence speed. A simulation of motion, reproduction, mutation and natural selection artificial life algorithm is proposed for 3D path planning of UAV in complex static and dynamic environment. By constructing a three-dimensional simulation environment that integrates static and dynamic obstacles, the fitness function of fusing path length and collision penalty is designed, and the sensitivity analysis of the core parameters (variation, survival, and reproduction) of the algorithm is carried out by combining cubic spline interpolation and bilinear difference, and the influence of parameters on the path planning quality and convergence speed is studied, and the parameter combination is determined to effectively balance the global path efficiency and local obstacle avoidance ability. This study provides reliable algorithm scheme and data support for real-time autonomous path planning of UAVs in complex dynamic scenarios, and also lays the foundation for multi-UAV collaborative planning.

title: EFL Learners’ Acceptance of a Game-Based Digital Platform for Grammar Learning: A Technology Acceptance Study
authors: Nuong To Huynh
publish: 2026 15th International Conference on Educational and Information Technology (ICEIT)
abstract: The aim of the study was to investigate EFL (English as a Foreign Language) students’ acceptance of using Wordwall platform in learning Grammar at a private university in The Mekong Delta of Vietnam. This study examines students’ acceptance in terms of perceived ease of use, perceived usefulness, and behavioral intention to use. The mixed-method design was used in this study. The participants of the study were 103 EFL students attending in English preparation courses which were compulsory in their university curriculum. The SPSS 25 program was used to analyze the data. The study employed a five-point Likert scale questionnaire and a semi-structured interview for data collection. The findings showed that students’ perceptions on the use of Wordwall platform in their grammar classes were positive. This study also explores what motivates student’s acceptance of using this platform to learn grammar. Implications for practical platforms of Wordwall were addressed in this study to enhance EFL students’ grammatical knowledge.

title: RecognAVSE-V2: An Improved Cross-Attention-Based Audio-Visual Speech Enhancement Approach
authors: Leandro A. Passos, João R. Manesco, Rahma Fourati, João P. Papa, Amir Hussain
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Complex Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICCSIC)
abstract: Audio-visual speech enhancement (AVSE) is a sub-field of machine learning that aims to improve speech quality from noisy audio signals, leveraging visual cues to guide information flow and enhance learning. In this context, the COG-MHEAR program supports an early competition, namely the AVSE Challenge (AVSEC), to advance the development of new solutions and AVSE methods while fostering the community. This paper introduces the RecognAVSE-V2, an improved and more efficient version of RecognAVSE proposed at AVSEC 2024, that implements a Time-Synced Cross-Attention Mechanism that models the temporal dependencies between both modalities. Its architecture consists of a video encoder for spatiotemporal feature learning, an audio encoder operating on STFT representations, and a time-synchronized cross-attention module that aligns audio and video features. Experimental results conducted over AVSE Challenge and CHiME3 datasets show RecognAVSE-V2 is capable of outperforming the baseline and its prior version in some cases while consisting of a model with reduced complexity, 20 times smaller, whose inference is 26% faster, and training demands a quarter of the total time.

title: Leveraging Self-Supervised Learning as Features in Audio Deepfake Detection
authors: Jean Alex Firmino Pinhata, Douglas Rodrigues, Alex M. G. de Almeida, Kelton Costa, João Paulo Papa
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Complex Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICCSIC)
abstract: This paper investigates the relative effectiveness of traditional acoustic features and self-supervised learning (SSL) embeddings for audio deepfake detection on the Fake-or-Real (FoR) corpus. We evaluate nine feature sets — MFCC, CQCC, RMS, ZCR, Teager, Wav2Vec2, HuBERT, WavLM, and Whisper, using Accuracy, F1-score, and Equal Error Rate (EER) as metrics. At the family level, Handcrafted and SSL features exhibit very similar distributions; quartiles and Mann–Whitney U tests reveal no statistically significant differences in Accuracy, F1, or EER (p > 0.90), indicating that SSL embeddings do not globally outperform handcrafted representations.At the feature level, however, clear patterns emerge, Whisper and MFCC are the most reliable features, with median accuracies of 0.9602 and 0.9571 and median EER of 0.0375 and 0.0391, respectively, without significant differences between them. WavLM forms a second tier with competitive but weaker results. Overall, the results show that cepstral features such as MFCC and CQCC remain robust options and that SSL embeddings, particularly Whisper and WavLM, complement rather than replace well-designed handcrafted features in audio deepfake detection.

title: Analyzing Usage Patterns of Artificial Intelligence Tools Among Criminology Students Using Machine Learning and Behavioral Analytics
authors: Hyrizel Banhi Umhao, Jordan L. Mariano, Isaac Angelo Dioses
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: This study analyzes the usage patterns of Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools among criminology students through the integration of behavioral analytics, sentiment analysis, and machine learning techniques. A dataset consisting of 533 respondents from Region II and Region III was collected using a structured survey instrument with Likert-scale and open-ended responses. Feature engineering was applied to construct composite behavioral indices, while sentiment analysis was performed to extract attitudinal insights from textual data. The processed dataset was used to train and evaluate classification models, including Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN). The results show that the SVM model achieved the highest performance, with an accuracy exceeding 95%, precision reaching 100%, and recall values up to 100% for the Moderate class. The LDA model also demonstrated strong performance with AUC values ranging from 0.983 to 0.997, while KNN showed comparatively lower performance with recall values as low as 69% for the High class. Feature importance analysis using the mRMR algorithm identified the AI Usage Index (0.3682) as the most significant predictor, followed by usage frequency and tools used. These findings highlight the effectiveness of integrating machine learning and sentiment analysis in modeling AI usage behavior and supporting data-driven educational strategies

title: SmartNav: Natural Language-Based Interaction with MapRoutes
authors: Prashanna Kumar S, Rajesh G, Navaneetha Krishnan U, Vijaya Lakshmi A
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: Traditional navigation systems are very good at what they were originally designed to do: compute routes that minimize time or distance. The problem is that this narrow focus does not always translate into a satisfying experience for real users navigating real cities. Urban road networks are layered and irregular, shaped by flyovers, tunnels, service roads, and temporary constraints that are difficult to flatten into a simple graph. When navigation systems fail to account for these structures, the result is often vague or confusing instructions, which in turn increase cognitive load and user frustration.This paper introduces SmartNav, an intelligent navigation assistant that attempts to close this gap by treating communication and structure as first-class concerns rather than afterthoughts. SmartNav tightly integrates Natural Language Understanding (NLU), structurally aware graph-based routing, and context-sensitive Natural Language Generation (NLG). Users can express nuanced preferences in everyday language, while the routing engine explicitly models multi-level urban infrastructure during both route computation and instruction generation. The aim is not only to reach a destination efficiently, but to explain the route in a way that aligns with how people actually perceive and describe their surroundings.We evaluate SmartNav using real-world road networks from the Chennai Metropolitan Area, a setting that captures many of the complexities typical of large, rapidly evolving cities. The system achieves an intent classification accuracy of 89.2 percent and satisfies user-specified constraints in 94.3 percent of cases. Complex natural language query interpretation achieves a success rate of 82.6 percent. Participants report an average satisfaction score of 4.3 out of 5, suggesting that the generated instructions are both clear and trustworthy. Cognitive burden reduction, measured through a validated NASA-TLX workload scale, showed a 31.4 percent decrease compared to conventional navigation interfaces. The end-to-end system maintains a low average response latency of 1.8 seconds. Taken together, these results suggest that SmartNav delivers navigation that is not only accurate, but also interpretable and better aligned with human expectations in complex urban environments.

title: A Hybrid Metaheuristic Approach to the VRPTW in Home Healthcare Logistics
authors: Ahlem Fkiri, Neila Rjaibi, Mohamed Salim Amri Sakhri
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Complex Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICCSIC)
abstract: The Vehicle Routing Problem with Time Windows (VRPTW) is a well-known challenge in transport logistics, particularly critical in home healthcare services where adhering to patient-specific time constraints is essential. This paper presents a practical and computationally efficient hybrid approach that integrates classical optimization techniques with lightweight artificial intelligence methods. Our methodology begins with K-means clustering to group patients based on their time availability, creating logical clusters for scheduling. Within each cluster, initial routes are generated using the NN algorithm, which are then refined through TS(Tabu search) to further minimize travel distance and improve adherence to time windows. This three-phase approach balances solution quality with computational simplicity, making it especially suitable for scenarios where operational resources and processing time are constrained. Experimental results on simulated datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves competitive route optimization while maintaining feasibility with respect to patient time windows. These results suggest strong potential for practical implementation in real-world home healthcare logistics and similar time-sensitive delivery environments.

title: AURA: A Diagnostic Framework for Tracking User Satisfaction of Interactive Planning Agents
authors: Takyoung Kim, Janvijay Singh, Shuhaib Mehri, Emre Can Acikgoz, Sagnik Mukherjee, Nimet Beyza Bozdag, Sumuk Shashidhar, Gokhan Tur, Dilek Hakkani-Tür
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: The growing capabilities of large language models (LLMs) in instruction-following and context-understanding have led to the emergence of agentic systems with numerous applications. Among these, task planning agents have become especially prominent in realistic scenarios involving complex internal pipelines, such as context understanding, tool management, and response generation. However, existing benchmarks predominantly evaluate agent performance based on task completion as a proxy for overall effectiveness. We hypothesize that merely improving task completion is misaligned with maximizing user satisfaction, as users interact with the entire agentic process and not just the end result. To address this gap, we propose <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">AURA</small>, an <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">A</u>gent-<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">U</u>ser inte<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">R</u>action <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">A</u>ssessment framework that conceptualizes the behavioral stages of interactive task planning agents. <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">AURA</small> offers a comprehensive assessment of agents through a set of atomic LLM evaluation criteria, allowing researchers and practitioners to diagnose specific strengths and weaknesses within the agent's decision-making pipeline. Our analyses show that agents excel at different behavioral stages, with user satisfaction shaped by both outcomes and intermediate behaviors. We also highlight future directions, including systems that leverage multiple agents and the limitations of user simulators in task planning.

title: Research on Adaptive Teaching for Students in the Initial Primary School Art Class—AIGC-Assisted Teaching Practice in the "Introducing Myself" Lesson
authors: Meng Jia, He Gao, Xiuli Sun, Jingting Ma
publish: 2026 15th International Conference on Educational and Information Technology (ICEIT)
abstract: Student adaptability plays a crucial role in establishing subject cognition and interest. The first primary school art class serves as a critical starting point for students' artistic learning journey. In response to widespread student adaptation challenges, this study proposes a shift in teaching from passively addressing problems to proactively nurturing potential. An adaptive teaching framework encompassing three dimensions — emotional ice-breaking, behavioral norm-setting, and relational connection-building—is constructed. Subsequently, teaching practices assisted by AIGC in the "Introducing Myself" lesson are implemented, thereby transforming potential "adaptation crisis points" in the first art class into "developmental opportunities".

title: Comprehensive Strain Study of Monolithic Nanosheet CFETs
authors: Ying-Qi Liu, Hung-Chun Chou, Tao Chou, Xuan-Hao Wu, Bo-Wei Huang, Chien-Te Tu, Yi-Chun Liu, C. W. Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Electron Devices
abstract: The step-by-step evolution of strain<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\textit {xx}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> along the current direction of monolithic nanosheet (NS) complementary FET (CFET) is studied through finite element analysis (FEA). The defective merge of source/drain regrowth is modeled by an air gap, while the defect-free merge of S/D regrowth is modeled by seamless S/D.
