Unlike existing methods learning only from miRNA-disease networks, BKAMDA leverages knowledge graphs to delineate distinct association types. By simulating informational propagation within knowledge graphs across diverse miRNA-disease relationships, the model investigates latent connections across various relationship types. Comparative analysis with competitive baselines using real-world experimentally validated datasets demonstrates excellent performance across multiple metrics. Three disease case studies further confirm model accuracy and effectiveness for precision medicine applications. Our knowledge-aware approach significantly advances miRNA-disease association prediction by capturing semantic diversity in biological interactions

title: Modeling and Optimization of a Multi-Mode Soft Crawling Robot Based on Giant Electrorheological Valves for Environmental Adaptability
authors: Tiantian Huang, Denan Xu, Cong Li, Yinuo Chen, Hengpeng Xie
publish: 2026 International Conference on Robot Perception and Intelligent Control (RPIC)
abstract: Soft robots have attracted significant attention due to their excellent compliance and environmental adaptability. However, achieving diverse motion patterns while simplifying the system structure remains a core challenge in this field. In recent years, flexible valves based on giant electrorheological fluids have been widely applied in soft robotics, owing to their advantages such as low energy consumption, fast response, continuously adjustable pressure, and good sealing performance. This paper proposes a multi-mode soft crawling robot with integrated actuation and control based on giant electrorheological fluids. The robot utilizes a soft grid-type actuator as its main body, with two giant electrorheological fluid-based flexible valves embedded internally. By controlling the combination of on/off states of these flexible valves, four distinct crawling modes are achieved. In the modeling process, the coupling effect between the actuator and the controller is fully considered, and a structural parameter optimization method aimed at maximizing crawling efficiency is proposed. This effectively balances the trade-off between the high pressure-holding performance required of the controller and the high driving efficiency required of the actuator within the integrated actuation-and-control structure. To meet the demands of different application scenarios, differentiated motion mode schemes are designed to ensure smooth and efficient passage of the robot under various operating conditions. Experimental results demonstrate that the optimized robot can stably realize the four preset crawling modes, providing a new technological pathway for adaptive locomotion of soft robots in complex environments.

title: Big Data Analytics Proven Smarter with Quantum Computing: A Framework for Optimizing Data at Scale
authors: K Dhanalakshmi, Mohammad Kanan, P. Hanumantha Rao, S. Bhuvana, V. Manasa, Balpreet Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing (ICONIC)
abstract: The need for efficient and scalable big data analytics frameworks is driven by the exponential growth of data in areas such as social media, the internet of things (IoT), finance, healthcare, and scientific simulations. While powerful, conventional HPC and distributed processing systems are illequipped to optimize and discover patterns in complicated, large datasets. The use of superposition and entanglement in quantum computing alters optimization and data processing. This research develops a hybrid quantum-classical paradigm for big data analytics by combining machine learning with quantum-assisted optimization. By integrating quantum algorithms such as variational optimization and quantum search with conventional distributed storage and preprocessing systems, we can enhance clustering, feature selection, and predictive modeling. Effective use of quantum-classical interfaces has been demonstrated for data encoding, parameter tuning, and iterative optimization. Processing is simplified, convergence is accelerated, and analytical accuracy for highdimensional datasets is enhanced by the suggested architecture. According to performance simulations, classical methods might not be as effective as combinatorial optimization and large-scale pattern recognition. According to this research, next-gen data-driven businesses can benefit greatly from quantum-enhanced analytics’ scalable, intelligent, and quick optimization mechanisms, which can completely transform big data processing.

title: Videolet-Based Multi-Feature Aggregation Framework for Face Anti-Spoofing in Biometric Systems
authors: P Poornima, M. Tamilselvi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing (ICONIC)
abstract: Anti-spoofing (AS) is one of the crucial criterions against attacks on biometric schemes. Numerous AS methods presented by various researchers often face challenge that leads to misclassification. This paper offerings a robust multifeature aggregation based face AS framework for detecting attacks in video sequences. Initially, the input videos are collected from a benchmark dataset and are segmented into multiple videolets to localize face and full-frame regions. These regions are then processed through a feature extraction pipeline that captures both handcrafted and deep features. Then, videolet score aggregation is performed with evidence fusion strategies to integrate regional and feature-level aggregation scores, enhancing the model’s decision-making capability. For classification, a Improved EfficientNet-B4 is employed to differentiate between genuine and spoofed faces. To optimize performance, the model’s hyperparameters are fine-tuned using a meta-heuristic Hippopotamus Optimization (HO) algorithm. Finally, spoof detection is achieved through score-level aggregation across all videolets and comparing the final score against a threshold value that helps in taking suitable decision. Experimental outcomes demonstrate the efficiency of the planned system in accurately identifying spoofing attempts across various attack scenarios by achieving an increased accuracy of $98.6 \%$ with reduced ACER of $2.1 \%$, APCER of $\mathbf{1 . 8} \boldsymbol{\%}$ and BPCER of $\mathbf{2 . 4} \boldsymbol{\%}$, respectively.

title: Hardware and Localization Analysis of Low-Power LoRaWAN GPS Tracker for Transport Applications
authors: S Vana Bagavath Pandiyan, A Aathi Bala, S Sriram, M Monish, T Karthika, L Hubert Mary
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing (ICONIC)
abstract: The use case for the development of a low-power GPS tracking device based on the LoRaWAN protocol includes logistics and transportation services. This paper presents a study of hardware and localization using CNN methods to improve reliability and increase the localization accuracy of the data collected. The suggested system uses microcontrollers with low energy consumption, LoRaWAN communication protocol, and GPS receiver to provide a low-power and long-distance connection. In the mobile application domain, the evaluation criteria of the hardware performance include power consumption, communication delay, and robustness. The effect of GPS error on the results can be compensated for by applying models built using CNNs. Experimental findings show that accuracy of localization and battery life is improved, relative to conventional GPS-only methods. The suggested tracker provides an easy to scale and affordable approach to real-time transport tracking, asset tracking, and smart logistics control in low-power wide-area networks.

title: MGA-UNet: A Lightweight Visual Segmentation Network for Industrial Multi-Scale Workpieces
authors: Jiajing Cheng, Shuangshuang Wu, Wenbai Chen, Lincheng Feng
publish: 2026 International Conference on Robot Perception and Intelligent Control (RPIC)
abstract: To address the complex interference in industrial multi-scale workpiece segmentation and localization, as well as the insufficient lightweight design of existing methods, this paper proposes a lightweight network named MGA-UNet. The network adopts the MobileNetV4 universal inverted bottleneck module as the encoder, and incorporates information-integrated attention skip connections together with the PagFM adaptive feature fusion module to generate high-accuracy foreground masks. Experiments on a self-built dataset demonstrate that MGA-UNet achieves 90.33% mIoU and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$95.65 {\%} \text{mAP}$</tex> with only 4.23 million parameters and 9.04 GFLOPs. Compared with the standard U-Net, it reduces parameters by 83.0%, decreases computation by 98.7%, and accelerates inference by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.93 \times$</tex>, enabling efficient deployment for industrial vision applications.

title: A Lightweight Model for Pedestrian Detection in Hazy Weather Based on Improved RT-DETR
authors: Zhiyue Gao, Junjun Chen
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Electronic Technology, Computers and Software Engineering (AETCSE)
abstract: Effective pedestrian detection across diverse environments plays a crucial role in enhancing the driving safety of autonomous vehicles. However, images captured under hazy conditions often suffer from reduced visibility and blurred pedestrian contours and appearances, which significantly impairs the efficacy of current detection frameworks. In response to these issues, we propose a lightweight Hazy Pedestrian Detection Transformer (HP-DETR) built upon Real-Time Detection Transformer (RT-DETR). Specifically, frequency–spatial aggregation network (FSAN) is proposed to aggregate global frequency information and spatial structural details, thereby enhancing foreground perception while improving inference efficiency. Furthermore, adaptive sparse self-attention (ASSA) mechanism is embedded within the Transformer encoder, enabling reduced computational cost and enhanced interaction between local and global features. Lastly, the multi-path block fusion (MPFusion) is introduced to aggregate multi-branch features for local-global semantic synergy. This mechanism maintains feature integrity within intricate backgrounds without compromising processing efficiency. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed model reaches a 92.9% mean Average Precision (mAP<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">50</inf>) on the proposed hazy pedestrian dataset. Relative to RT-DETR, our approach boosts mAP<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">50</inf> by 1.1% , simultaneously decreasing the number of parameters and computational complexity (measured in FLOPs) by 35.5% and 32.9%, respectively. In summary, HP-DETR establishes an effective tradeoff between detection accuracy and inference efficiency, demonstrating strong potential for real-time pedestrian detection under hazy weather conditions.

title: Research on Automatic Assembly Method for Multihole Shaft Fitting Using Mirror-Imaging-Based Pose Recognition and Robot Visual Servoing
authors: Chengjia Weng, Xutang Zhang, Xianglin Bai, Tiangong Jin
publish: 2026 International Conference on Robot Perception and Intelligent Control (RPIC)
abstract: Visual occlusion caused by the end-effector is a major challenge in robotic automated assembly. To address this problem, this paper proposes a mirror-assisted visual servoing method for high-precision printed circuit board (PCB) assembly under severe occlusion. A planar mirror is introduced to create a virtual observation viewpoint, enabling occlusion-free 6-DOF pose estimation of the grasped PCB based on mirror imaging geometry and a PnP algorithm. A hybrid visual servoing strategy is then adopted, combining mirror-based coarse positioning with imagebased fine alignment. Experiments conducted on a robotic platform with a Basler camera demonstrate that the final position and orientation errors are approximately 0.1 mm, meeting the required accuracy. The proposed method provides a reliable and low-cost solution for precision assembly in vision-constrained environments.

title: A Thyristor-Based Controllable Energy Dissipation Device for Flexible Low-Frequency AC Transmission Systems
authors: Yingpei Wang, Gaoshan Hou, Yuwei Li, Xiaoyu Zhang, Bao Liu, Yunfei Xu
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technology (EECT)
abstract: This paper presents a novel centralized AC controllable energy dissipation device specifically designed for large-scale renewable energy integration via low-frequency AC transmission systems. The device adopts a three-phase deltaconnected topology consisting of resistors in series with antiparallel thyristors, connected to the converter station AC bus through a coupling transformer. By employing thyristor phaseangle control, the device achieves continuous and smooth regulation of dissipation power. A three-layer hierarchical control architecture is developed, integrating system-level, device-level, and valve-level control. The device-level controller uniquely combines lookup-table-based open-loop control with a PI regulator, which significantly enhances control accuracy and dynamic performance. Simulation results conclusively validate the effectiveness of the proposed strategy, demonstrating that the device rapidly absorbs surplus power during system faults, thereby effectively maintaining power balance and ensuring the secure and stable operation of low-frequency AC transmission systems.

title: Automatic Detection of Cutting Paths for Crane Hook Flashing Based on Line Laser 3D Point Clouds
authors: Shuaiwen Liu, Shiran Tang, Cunrong Li, Hanmin Peng
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Manufacturing and Automation (IMA)
abstract: To address the subjectivity of manual operation, low precision, and poor adaptability to complex curved surfaces in traditional crane hook flash cutting, this paper proposes an automated path extraction method utilizing 3D line laser scanning. The proposed pipeline integrates coarse-to-fine point cloud registration via RANSAC and Iterative Closest Point algorithms, followed by geometric completion using guidance-field-based Poisson reconstruction. A hierarchical boundary extraction strategy is introduced, combining distance constraints for initial screening with arch extremum detection for precise localization. Experimental results demonstrate that the method exhibits strong robustness against process noise, achieving a boundary extraction recall rate of 99.15%, which outperforms the traditional <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha$</tex>-shape method by 26.1 percentage points.

title: Transconductance Matching Optimization for Rail-to-Rail Input Class-AB Linear Amplifier Based on Level Shifting and Redundant Differential Pair
authors: Hongcheng Huang, Dawei Liu, Jianlin Fan, Wei Zhuang, Xingfu Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Electronic Technology, Computers and Software Engineering (AETCSE)
abstract: This paper presents a rail-to-rail input, class-AB output linear amplifier designed for ultraviolet photoelectric signal acquisition. A transconductance matching technique employing level shifting and a redundant current mirror is adopted. The operational amplifier comprises a rail-to-rail input stage, a translinear loop intermediate stage, and a class-AB output stage. To address the transconductance (gm) variation caused by supply voltage fluctuations in conventional DC level-shifting rail-to-rail amplifiers, a transconductance matching scheme utilizing a level-shifting redundant differential pair current mirror is proposed. This achieves transconductance linearization over a common-mode input range of 0-1.8V, with a transconductance variation error ∆gm/gm of less than 2.4%. The proposed rail-to-rail class-AB operational amplifier is implemented in a 0.18µm CMOS process with a 1.8 V power supply. Post-layout simulation results demonstrate that under a 0.9V common-mode input voltage with a 0.9Vpp swing, the amplifier achieves a THD of - 83dB, a loop gain of 153dB, a phase margin of 80°, and a unity-gain bandwidth of 23MHz.

title: A Multi-Factor Biometric Document Authorization System
authors: J Robin Nickson, U Rajesh, H Niroshini Infantia, E Christopher Siddarth
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing (ICONIC)
abstract: This paper presents a contactless multimodal biometric authentication system that integrates facial recognition, speaker verification, and spoken passphrase validation to enhance the reliability of traditional single-factor authentication methods. The system is implemented in Python and leverages ArcFace for facial feature extraction and PyAnnote for speaker embedding generation from real-time webcam and microphone inputs. The extracted embeddings are matched against enrolled templates using threshold-based similarity metrics, and access is granted only when all biometric modalities satisfy predefined criteria. The proposed system further enables secure document authorization by automatically applying a digital signature to PDF files upon successful authentication. Experimental evaluation was conducted on a controlled dataset under varying lighting and background noise conditions, demonstrating promising verification accuracy for both facial and voice modalities. The system is designed to be scalable and can be extended with additional security features such as liveness detection and secure cloud-based storage for high-security applications.

title: Multiphysics Simulation Study on Tension Monitoring of ACSR Conductors Based on the Magnetoelastic Effect
authors: Jiahui Wang, Tianyu Liu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Manufacturing and Automation (IMA)
abstract: Aluminum Conductor Steel Reinforced (ACSR) is the core load-bearing component of overhead transmission lines, and its tension state is directly related to the safe and stable operation of the power grid. This study proposes a non-destructive testing method based on the magnetoelastic effect for the tension monitoring of ACSR conductors. Using the COMSOL Multiphysics software, an axisymmetric finite element model was established, incorporating a soft iron magnetizer, an excitation coil, a sensing coil, and the conductor under test. Through coupled calculations of the electromagnetic field and solid mechanics modules, the influence mechanism of conductor tension variation on the measurement signal is analyzed. Simulation results indicate that variations in the overall conductor tension alter the average magnetic permeability of the steel core via the magnetoelastic effect. This leads to corresponding changes in the total magnetic flux through the magnetic circuit, which consequently affects the induced voltage signal in the sensing coil. The theoretical feasibility of monitoring ACSR wire tension based on the magnetostrictive effect is verified, providing a theoretical model and basis for developing an online monitoring system. This study serves as a simulation validation, with subsequent work focusing on experiments and practical applications.

title: A Hierarchical Comprehensive Evaluation Framework for UAV Object Detectors via Probabilistic Inversion and Adaptive Fusion
authors: Chenyu Zhao
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Electronic Technology, Computers and Software Engineering (AETCSE)
abstract: Existing evaluations of UAV object detection models mainly focus on limited performance metrics, making it difficult to assess models comprehensively across accuracy, efficiency, and robustness. To address this issue, we propose a hierarchical evaluation framework based on probabilistic inversion and adaptive fusion. The framework integrates expert knowledge and objective data to construct a multi-dimensional evaluation system with 14 indicators. Experiments on the YOLO11 series show that YOLO11l achieves the highest overall score, demonstrating balanced performance across usability, efficiency, and robustness.

title: BLAR-3D: Beta-Regulated Learning-Based Adaptive Routing for 3D Network-on-Chip
authors: Cevdet Aslan, Suleyman Tosun
publish: 2026 IEEE 29th International Symposium on Design and Diagnostics of Electronic Circuits and Systems (DDECS)
abstract: Network-on-Chip (NoC) architectures are widely used in many-core systems due to their scalability. Threedimensional NoCs further improve performance by stacking multiple layers using Through-Silicon Vias (TSVs), at the cost of increased routing complexity. Deterministic routing algorithms often suffer from congestion under non-uniform traffic, motivating adaptive solutions. In this paper, we present a learningbased adaptive routing approach for 3D NoCs that stabilizes Q-learning-based routing decisions using a beta-regulated probabilistic selection mechanism. Direction-based learning is employed to reduce learning complexity. Experimental results show improvements in latency and throughput compared to deterministic and conventional adaptive routing schemes.

title: Lightweight Large-Model Inference with Privacy Protection for Edge IoT Systems
authors: Xiwang Ma
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Electronic Technology, Computers and Software Engineering (AETCSE)
abstract: Edge IoT systems increasingly require real-time intelligent services, yet direct deployment of large language models (LLMs) or multimodal foundation models on constrained edge devices faces severe memory, latency, and privacy risks. Cloud-only inference pipelines can simplify deployment but often violate data minimization requirements in sensitive scenarios such as healthcare monitoring and smart homes. This paper proposes a privacy-preserving lightweight large-model inference framework for edge IoT systems that jointly optimizes model compression, collaborative inference, and secure execution. The framework combines mixed-precision quantization, structured pruning, and knowledge distillation to derive compact edge students, while a federated inference coordinator exchanges only protected intermediate statistics rather than raw data. Differential privacy is introduced for controllable leakage mitigation, and trusted execution environments (TEEs) are used to isolate security-critical operators and keys. A latency-aware policy engine dynamically selects local-only, edge-only, or edge-cloud fallback paths according to device load and privacy budget. Experiments on a heterogeneous edge cluster show that the proposed method reduces model storage by over 80% and cuts end-to-end inference latency by up to 75% compared with an FP16 baseline while maintaining competitive accuracy under strict privacy constraints. The results demonstrate practical feasibility for privacy-sensitive edge intelligence upgrades.

title: SimDESC: A Privacy-Preserving Intrusion Detection Framework for Encrypted Network Traffic Using Machine Learning and Deep Learning
authors: Veda Abdhija Koka, Sai Lakshmi Bobbara, G Divya Sri, Vardhan A, Narasimham Challa
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: The widespread adoption of end-to-end encryption renders traditional deep packet inspection (DPI) ineffective, leaving traffic metadata exposed to side channel analysis, while simultaneously concealing malicious payloads from network defenders. This paper presents SimDESC: Simple Deep Learning-Based Encryption for Stealth Communication, a unified three-layered framework that resolves the fundamental conflict between user privacy and network security. The framework integrates: (1) a Content aware endpoint security module using Random Forest to block malware before encryption; (2) a Stealth privacy Module employing AES 256 encryption and Differentially Private Shaper (DPS) to obfuscate traffic metadata; and (3) an Intelligent Detection Module using an Information Bottleneck Convolutional Neural Network (IB-CNN) to detect encrypted threats without decryption. The IB-CNN is trained on the CICIDS-2017 dataset for detecting DoS attacks. Evaluations conducted on DPS-obfuscated traffic using an 80/20 train-test split demonstrate a stable 89.65% detection accuracy, with a precision of 0.93, recall of 0.86, F1-score of 0.89, and a false positive rate of 0.07, outperforming the Random Forest baseline (85.4%) under obfuscated traffic conditions. The system demonstrably masks traffic patterns to resemble benign streaming activity.

title: Fault Detection in Solar Plates Using Deep Learning
authors: Zoha Memon, Muhammad Haseeb, Sammer Zai
publish: 2025 8th International MultiTopic ICT Conference (IMTIC)
abstract: This paper explores the use of recent deep learning technology for detection of faults in solar panels based on thermal images and thereby the contribution of cutting-edge technologies toward enhancing the efficiency and reliability of renewable energy systems. The research relies on the development of a robust system that identifies faults in solar panels and improves the efficiency and longevity of solar installations. The research evaluates the performance of different state-of-the-art object detection models, such as YOLOv9, YOLOv10, and R-50 Faster RCNN deployed on the Detectron2 framework, in the thermal anomaly detection. The results of experiments show immense variations in performance where the best accuracy is $92 \%$ for YOLOv10, followed by YOLOv9 with $83 \%$ and R-50 Faster R-CNN with $79 \%$. This thorough analysis can therefore comment on the pros and cons of such algorithms in actual use, specifically concerning their accuracyperformance trade-offs. It culminates in providing a web application where users submit pictures of their solar panels, and these pictures are given detailed reports in relation to the condition of their solar panels. This app enhances user interaction as it provides identified fault images with labels and a summary of important information on solar panel status. Overall, the results emphasize the enormous potential of combining advanced machine learning technology with solar panel inspection for the development of improved maintenance approaches and anticipatory solar energy resource management.

title: A Random Forest Tobacco Moisture Content Prediction Method Based on Improved Particle Swarm Optimization
authors: Yi Zhang, Jie Qian, Zhijin Liu, GuanZhun Cao, Xiaofei Wang, Kejia Feng, Liu Yang, Hai Feng
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Electronic Technology, Computers and Software Engineering (AETCSE)
abstract: The frequent disturbances in the operating conditions of the leaf drying process, the strong coupling of variables, and the significant measurement noise make the prediction of outlet moisture content face problems such as feature redundancy, model instability, and high cost of manual parameter adjustment in engineering applications. To improve prediction accuracy, this paper proposes an improved particle swarm optimization-based random forest water content prediction method (IPSO-RF).Correlation analysis was used to quantify the correlation between process variables and target moisture content, and autocorrelation variables that overlapped with the target location and variables with weak correlation were removed. With RMSE as the optimization objective, a stagnation detection triggering mechanism is introduced. When the population search stagnates, Cauchy mutation is applied to inferior particles to enhance their ability to escape local optima. This is combined with spiral path search to strengthen the local mining of the optimal neighborhood, thereby improving optimization efficiency and convergence stability. Comparative experiments show that IPSO-RF outperforms traditional RF and PSO-RF models in both convergence speed and prediction accuracy. In actual production data validation, the predicted curves are highly consistent with the measured results in the steady-state region, and the residual distribution shows no significant systematic deviation. The average ACC (±5% relative-error band) of four batches of stable production period experiments reaches 99.92%. In summary, IPSO-RF can effectively predict the moisture content at the yarn drying outlet, and still maintains good accuracy and robustness under complex working conditions.

title: Virtual Avatar Gaze and Face Pressure in L2 Listening: A Sociopragmatic Perspective on Metaverse Learning Environments
authors: Haokun Liu, Yating Zhang, Shenglan Chu
publish: 2026 15th International Conference on Educational and Information Technology (ICEIT)
abstract: Virtual avatars now commonly appear as instructors in metaverse-based educational videos. Most existing studies, drawing on theories of attention guidance, cognitive load, and social presence, treat avatar gaze as a helpful cue that directs learners’ attention during L2 tasks. Drawing on Goffman’s face-work theory, Brown and Levinson’s politeness framework, and observations from metaverse learning materials, this paper offers a different view. We argue that when avatars maintain sustained gaze during demanding L2 listening activities, they can unintentionally create face threats. These threats, filtered through learners’ subjective interpretations and cultural backgrounds, generate what we call "face pressure". From a sociopragmatic standpoint, this pressure shows up in three ways: what looks like attentional avoidance actually functions as a negative face strategy, and what appears as difficulties with speech segmentation reflects a need for face-relief. We position "interactional dignity" as a key consideration for inclusive design. Bringing together theoretical foresight and design ethics, this paper responds to ongoing conversations about accessibility in metaverse learning spaces. In doing so, it opens new ground for dialogue between sociopragmatics and educational technology while offering fresh angles for understanding L2 listening in immersive settings.

title: Preclinical Assessment of an Image-Guided Histotripsy System for Renal Ablation_supp4-3692044.mp4
authors: Muskan Singh
publish: N/A

title: Fast Parameter Extraction of an Extended Jiles–Atherton Model Using a Physics–Constrained CNN–KAN Framework
authors: Bohua Zhang, Martin Pfost
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: Accurate hysteresis modeling is essential for high–frequency magnetic components, and the Jiles–Atherton (JA) model is widely used for this purpose. However, parameter identification remains computationally demanding, as traditional optimization approaches require iterative fitting of individual hysteresis loops (typically 1–2 minutes per loop) and scale poorly to large datasets. To address this limitation, a physics–constrained convolutional neural network and Kolmogorov–Arnold Network (CNN–KAN) framework is proposed for fast parameter extraction of a delay–enhanced JA model. The proposed method directly takes the excitation waveform H(t) as input and incorporates experimentally measurable hysteresis features into a multi–objective loss function to enforce physical consistency. Using measured data ranging from 300 kHz to 1MHz from a TDK N49 toroidal ferrite core for training, the model achieves accurate reconstruction of hysteresis loops and reliable prediction of JA parameters within a single forward pass. Comparative evaluations against conventional optimization–based methods and alternative neural network architectures demonstrate a substantial reduction in computation time while maintaining high modeling accuracy. These results indicate that the proposed framework offers a scalable and efficient solution for high–frequency hysteresis modeling with the delay-enhanced JA model.

title: TIME: A Taylor-Inspired Mixed-Effects Model for IDH Prediction_supp3-3693918.pdf
authors: Pengwei Hu
publish: N/A
abstract: Background: Intradialytic hypotension (IDH) is a critical complication in hemodialysis that increases morbidity and treatment risks, yet existing machine learning methods inadequately address session-level physiological state heterogeneity and fail to balance linear and nonlinear feature interactions, limiting predictive accuracy and interpretability. Methods: We developed TIME (Taylor-Inspired Mixed-Effects Model), which explicitly separates linear interactions, higher-order nonlinear dependencies, and residual terms inspired by Taylor series expansion, while incorporating a physiological state embedding layer to capture session-level physiological state heterogeneity revealed through hierarchical clustering on autoencoder-derived latent representations of hematologic indicators selected using SHAP analysis and clinical knowledge. Using data from 532 hemodialysis patients (18,309 records), we compared TIME against 19 state-of-the-art baseline models, including blood pressure-stratified subcohort and out-of-distribution analyses. Results: Hierarchical clustering identified two distinct hematological states with different IDH risks (57.93% vs. 55.75%, $p=6.60\times 10^{-7}$). TIME achieved 0.6929 accuracy, 0.7435 F1-score, and 0.3663 MCC on the full cohort, the highest AUC across all three subcohorts (0.7010, 0.6795, and 0.7201), and strong out-of-distribution performance (accuracy 0.7053, F1 0.7377, MCC 0.4009). Integrating TIME's architecture into baseline models improved MCC by 2.67 percentage points on average. Conclusions: TIME effectively predicts IDH by modeling session-level physiological state heterogeneity and balancing feature interactions, with robust generalizability across diverse populations. Significance: TIME advances precision medicine in dialysis care by enabling early risk identification and personalized treatment strategies, while revealing broader immune, nutritional, and fluid-related markers of IDH risk.

title: Tackling the Kidnapped Robot Problem via Sparse Feasible Hypothesis Sampling and Reliable Batched Multi-Stage Inference
authors: Muhua Zhang, Lei Ma, Ying Wu, Kai Shen, Deqing Huang, Henry Leung
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: This paper addresses the Kidnapped Robot Problem (KRP), a core localization challenge of relocalizing a robot in a known map without a prior pose estimate upon localization loss or at SLAM initialization. For this purpose, a passive 2-D global relocalization framework is proposed. It estimates the global pose efficiently and reliably from a single LiDAR scan and an occupancy grid map while the robot remains stationary, thereby enhancing the long-term autonomy of mobile robots. The proposed framework casts global relocalization as a non-convex problem and solves it using a multi-hypothesis scheme with batched multi-stage inference and early termination, balancing completeness and efficiency. The traversability-constrained Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT) asymptotically covers the reachable space and restricts the search space to traversable regions in the occupancy grid, generating sparse and uniformly distributed feasible positional hypotheses. The hypotheses are preliminarily ordered by the proposed Scan Mean Absolute Difference (SMAD), a coarse beam-error-level metric that enables efficient early termination by prioritizing high-likelihood candidates and is optimized for limited scan measurements. The Translation-Affinity Scan-to-Map Alignment Metric (TAM) is introduced for reliable orientation selection and accurate final pose evaluation, mitigating the degradation of conventional likelihood-field-based metrics under translational uncertainty, non-panoramic LiDAR scans, and environmental changes. Real-world experiments on a resource-constrained mobile robot with non-panoramic LiDAR scans in two representative indoor scenarios show that the proposed framework improves the mean relocalization success rate by about 26.9 percentage points over the strongest baseline, while reducing runtime from several seconds or even tens of seconds to the sub-second to 1.6s range in most comparable cases. These results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves higher success rates with substantially lower runtime under the same time constraint, validating its effectiveness as a practical relocalization module for laser SLAM.

title: IEEE Standard for Energy Efficient Dynamic Line Rate Transmission System
authors: N/A
publish: N/A
abstract: An energy-efficient, rate and modulation adaptive transmission system designed for the deployment of mixed line rates in optical networks is specified by this standard. The transmission system is introduced by this standard, and mechanisms to optimally configure the line rate to efficiently accommodate varying traffic loads while reducing the power consumption are provided.

title: Viscoelastic modeling of the magnetic losses in punch-degraded non-oriented electrical steel
authors: Benjamin Ducharne, Floran Martin, Anouar Belahcen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: This study examines the effect of punching on magnetic losses in non-oriented electrical steel using a viscoelastic model based on fractional-derivative operators. Specimens with identical material volume and increasing edge-affected area were characterized under alternating magnetization with controlled sinusoidal flux density. The frequency dependence of losses is described by a fractional order n and a coefficient ρ, related to viscoelastic mechanisms and material properties, respectively. The results show an almost monotonic increase of n with edge-affected area, providing a compact indicator of cutting-induced degradation, while ρ remains close to analytically predicted values. A scaling analysis links the experimental configurations to 1 kW transformers and motors. Loss predictions at elevated frequencies highlight the significant contribution of punching-induced degradation under practical operating conditions.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications

title: Knowledge Diffusion-Based Adaptive Alignment with Hierarchical Context for Video Temporal Grounding
authors: Ran Ran, Jiwei Wei, Yang Yang, Guoqing Wang, Kuien Liu, Jie Zou, Heng Tao Shen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Video Temporal Grounding (VTG) aims to localize the temporal moment that corresponds to a natural-language query in an untrimmed video. However, VTG faces two core challenges: narrowing the semantic gap between concise text queries and complex visual content, and extracting discriminative moment cues from long background-dominant sequences. To address these challenges, we propose Knowledge Diffusion Alignment (KDA), a diffusion-based framework that operates in the semantic feature domain. At its core, a Background Residual Diffusion Model (BRDM) starts from adaptive global video knowledge and, under query conditioning, progressively removes query-irrelevant background residuals to synthesize moment knowledge. In addition, to provide a strong conditional prior and multi-scale supervision, Hierarchical Video Knowledge Extraction (HVKE) aggregates context at multiple temporal granularities, producing both video and moment knowledge. KDA is trained in three stages: first learning target-aware moment knowledge, then aligning the diffusion process to that target, and finally performing end-to-end inference using BRDM-generated knowledge. The synthesized moment knowledge is injected into knowledge prompt reasoning, where a Dynamic Moment-aware Encoder (DME) adaptively generates dynamic temporal convolution kernels conditioned on the moment knowledge, coupling efficient local modeling with global self-attention. For prediction, a Spans-Enhanced Hierarchical Decoder (SEHD) constructs a temporal pyramid and adaptively selects the most relevant initial spans to exploit intrinsic alignment cues. Experiments across public benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed KDA outperforms the state-of-the-art methods, and extensive ablations further verify the effectiveness of the module design. The code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/Evangelion09/HKDA</uri>.

title: Evolving Diverse Red-team Language Models in Multi-round Multi-agent Games
authors: Chengdong Ma, Ziran Yang, Hai Ci, Jun Gao, Minquan Gao, Xuehai Pan, Yaodong Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: The primary challenge in deploying Large Language Model (LLM) is ensuring its harmlessness. Red team can identify vulnerabilities by attacking LLM to attain safety. However, current efforts heavily rely on single-round prompt designs and unilateral red team optimizations against fixed blue teams. These static approaches lead to significant reductions in generation diversity, known as the mode collapse, which makes it difficult to discover the potential risks in the increasingly complex human-LLM interactions. Here we introduce dynamic Red Team Game (RTG) to comprehensively analyze the multi-round offensive and defensive interactions between red team and blue team. Furthermore, we develop a Gamified Red Team Solver (GRTS) with diversity measures to mitigate mode collapse and theoretically guarantee the convergence of approximate Nash equilibrium which results in better strategies for both teams. Empirical results demonstrate that GRTS explore diverse and implicit attacks to adaptively exploit various LLMs, surpassing the constraints of specific modes. Insightfully, the geometrical structure we unveil of the red team task aligns with the spinning top hypothesis, confirming the necessity of constructing a diverse LLM population as a promising proxy for heterogeneous human expert red-teamers. This paves the way for scalable toxicity detection and safe alignment for LLMs.

title: Avian-Scale Flapping-Wing Air Vehicle With Independent Actuation Mechanism for Enhanced Lateral Maneuverability_supp1-3688743.pdf
authors: Jae-Hung Han
publish: N/A
abstract: Birds achieve remarkable aerial maneuverability through bilateral independent control of their wings, enabling agile flight in confined spaces and at low speeds. However, implementing such independent actuation in medium-to-heavy (>400 g) avian-scale flapping-wing air vehicles (FWAVs) has remained challenging due to power limitations of servo-driven approaches or unsynchronized phases in dual motor-linkage approaches. This article presents an independent actuation mechanism with encoder-based synchronization and validates its application to heavy avian-scale FWAVs through the 940 g KRoHawk-I platform. Each wing is driven by a separate actuation unit comprising a BLDC motor, gear train, and linkage mechanism. The actuation units are capable of rotation about the pitch axis to enable asymmetric stroke plane control. The synchronization controller maintains crank angle errors within 10° for stable flight, validated through ground tests. For lateral maneuvering, asymmetric stroke plane control generates differential angles of attack and trajectories between main wings, producing rolling moments nine times as large as those generated by tail control. Flight tests demonstrate significant maneuverability enhancement compared to tail control with symmetric stroke plane: 65% reduction in distance required for 90° turns and 70% reduction in roll response time.

title: Wavelet-enhanced Foundation Model Adaptation for Weakly Supervised Urban Tree Canopy Mapping_supp1-3693826.pdf
authors: Jingyu Yang
publish: N/A
abstract: Most existing weakly supervised methods for tree canopy segmentation suffer from limited accuracy due to insufficient supervision and poor generalization. We propose AMP—an end-to-end semantic segmentation framework that adaptively refines a pre-trained vision foundation model (DINOv2) via a lightweight CNN-based adapter. AMP comprises two key components: (1) a backbone that integrates high-frequency wavelet features with the foundation model through a novel High-Frequency Feature Integration (HFFI) module, and (2) a multi-branch pseudo-label generation module that produces diverse supervisory signals under sparse annotation. To mitigate the domain gap between DINOv2’s pretraining data and our remote sensing imagery, we design an adaptive adapter that fine-tunes the foundation model in a task-aware manner. Furthermore, to ensure pseudo-label reliability, we employ three parallel generation branches followed by a pixel-wise selection strategy to synthesize high-quality supervision. We have constructed a sparsely labeled dataset to enable and evaluate weakly supervised tree canopy segmentation in urban environments. Extensive results demonstrate that AMP achieves state-of-the-art performance under sparse supervision, significantly outperforming existing weakly supervised approaches. Which achieved an mIoU of 81.57% and an mF1 score of 89.43% on the UTC-sparse dataset. The code and data will be made publicly available at: https://github.com/Tengmingn/AMP.

title: An Air-Jet Continuum Robot for Industrial Integrated Fluidic Circuit Inspection_supp9-3682403.mp4
authors: Yongchang Zhang
publish: N/A
abstract: Industrial fluidic circuits operating under long-term extreme conditions are prone to defects such as cavitation, corrosion, and cracks, raising a critical demand for in-situ robotic inspection. Traditional manual disassembly-based methods are inefficient, while existing robots lack the ability to simultaneously adapt to complex geometries and maintain stable adsorption on surfaces of varying curvature. This study proposes an air-jet continuum robot (AJCR) that integrates three core modules: air-jet modules, a unit flexible continuum skeleton (UFCS), and stiffness-adjustable flexible suction cups (FSCs), enabling effective crawling and inspection inside integrated industrial fluid circuits. The UFCS provides omnidirectional bending capability of up to 196° enabling navigation through complex channels including abrupt turns of 120° inside stop valves. Under an input pressure of 0.8 MPa, the AJCR generates a maximum air-jet counter-thrust of 4.72 N for adsorption and 4.16 N for driving, ensuring reliable locomotion on unstructured, slippery, and discontinuous surfaces. Based on the sliding suction strategy, the FSC offers a maximum static adsorption force of 35.1 N, reduces sliding friction to 2.25 N on stainless steel, and adapts to curved surfaces with radii as small as 125 mm, facilitating stable sliding adsorption even across welded seams. Experimental results demonstrate that the AJCR smoothly navigates pipeline systems, enters stop valves, mixed-flow pumps and pressure vessels, and operates in liquid-filled environments, significantly improving inspection coverage and operational safety in fluidic delivery systems.

title: Hierarchical Diffusion for Sparse-to-Full Human Motion Reconstruction
authors: Fangyu Du, Yang Yang, Xuehao Gao, Hongye Hou, Chen Sun
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: Human motion generation from sparse observations is an ill-posed problem in AR/VR, where head-mounted devices often capture only head and wrist trajectories. Prior methods usually reconstruct full-body motion in a single stage, forcing inference over a vast solution space and producing inaccurate lower-body motion, weak temporal coherence, and implausible sequences that degrade avatar embodiment. We present MAGE, a Multi-stage Avatar GEnerator based on hierarchical diffusion. Instead of predicting 22-joint motion at once, MAGE progressively refines motion from a coarse 6-part representation to full joints. Each stage injects stage-specific motion priors and uses intermediate predictions to constrain subsequent refinement, reducing ambiguity and stabilizing dynamics. Experiments on large-scale motion datasets show that MAGE improves reconstruction accuracy, temporal smoothness, and perceptual realism over state-of-the-art baselines, enabling more reliable full-body animation from minimal AR/VR sensing while preserving real-time interaction.

title: Zero-Jitter Multi-Channel ASCON-AEAD128 Gateway on FPGA SoC for Safety-Critical IIoT
authors: Yourak Choi, Sangook Moon
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Safety-critical Industrial Internet of Things applications such as collaborative robots and motion control demand deterministic encryption latency that software-based gateways cannot guarantee due to operating system non-determinism. This paper presents a Field-Programmable Gate Array-based Industrial Internet of Things security gateway on an Intel Cyclone V System-on-Chip that processes ten sensor channels through a single ASCON-AEAD128 core using time-division multiplexing, fully compliant with National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-232 including 128-bit authentication tag generation for each sample. The architecture integrates four contributions, namely a near-zero-overhead hardware scheduler achieving one-to-two-cycle context switching, a time-division multiplexed ASCON-AEAD128 engine with per-sample authenticated encryption, a hardware Welford statistics module for real-time 3-sigma outlier detection with a 97% area reduction via an iterative divider, and a zero-copy direct memory access path providing a 21,000-fold read speedup over processor polling. Experiments on the Terasic DE1-SoC demonstrate all 168 Known Answer Test vectors passing, a constant encryption latency of 153 clock cycles (3.06 microseconds) with zero jitter across ten channels under hardware-managed sensor timing, and an end-to-end throughput of 399,545 entries per second (97.5 megabits per second) verified over 11.9 million entries with 100% decryption success and 99.97% authentication tag verification. The design occupies 43% of available Adaptive Logic Modules at 78.5 MHz, exceeding the 50 MHz target by 57%. These results show that hardware time-division multiplexed scheduling achieves deterministic latency equivalent to dedicated single-channel implementations with approximately 1.9 to 3.8 times lower per-channel cryptographic area, supporting worst-case execution time analysis required for safety-critical deployment.

title: Physics-Based Vision System for Handling and Inspection of Ultra-Thin Glass in Hydrodynamic Processes
authors: Kyung Hoon Kim, Jae Wook Jeon
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The mass production of Ultra-Thin Glass (UTG) faces significant hurdles in automated handling and inspection due to its transparency, residual moisture, and flexibility. While deep learning (DL) approaches offer high performance, they are often limited by computational latency and high hardware costs. This paper proposes a cost-effective, physics-based vision solution to address these bottlenecks, marking the industry’s first feasibility verification for mass production. The system integrates a meniscus-induced imaging technique to detect transparent UTG with 100% reliability and a moisture-resilient crack detection module. By optimizing stage surface roughness (Ra ≈ 5.0) and applying morphological filtering, the system distinguishes water droplets from cracks, reducing the Missed detection rate to 7.7% without heavy computation. Additionally, a Zone-based Alignment algorithm compensates for optical distortion and cassette tolerances to ensure precise robotic loading. Validation over 281 continuous cycles demonstrated a 100% detection rate, 0% breakage, and a rapid processing time of 377 ms. Notably, this rule-based approach reduces Capital Expenditure (CAPEX) by $34,560 per unit by eliminating the need for GPUs. The proposed solution provides a robust, economically viable standard for automating advanced display material manufacturing.

title: Multi Person Localization and Breathing Rate Estimation using Translational and Rotational Synthetic Aperture Radar
authors: Ashutosh Deshwal, Alireza Azizi, Shubham Sinha, Divyanshu Pandey, Nishant Mehrotra, Amitangshu Pal, Ashutosh Sabharwal
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Imaging
abstract: Reliable detection and localization of humans through structural obstructions is critical for time-sensitive applications such as disaster response. This paper presents a novel ultra-wideband (UWB) radar-based framework for multi-person localization and breathing rate estimation in obstructed environments. To overcome the challenges posed by signal attenuation, multipath interference, and limited spatial resolution, we propose a synthetic aperture radar (SAR) system that combines linear translation and on-axis rotation of a single radar. By fusing intensity maps from multiple radar viewpoints, the system achieves enhanced localization of human targets. A signal quality assessment pipeline, including SNR-based filtering, dominance ratio analysis, and periodicity checks, is used to extract clean respiratory signals from selected range bins. The estimates are further integrated into a spatial breathing-rate matrix, which is fused with radar motion maps to suppress false detections and improve reliability. The proposed algorithm is validated experimentally under line-of-sight, through-wall, and emulated rubble conditions. Results demonstrate significant improvements in localization accuracy and robustness of respiration-based human presence detection. The proposed post-processing filtering algorithms reduce the 80<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</sup> percentile Chamfer distance by 1.53×, while the maximum Chamfer distance is reduced by <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\sim 3\times$</tex-math></inline-formula>. The estimated breathing rate correlates strongly with ground truth while achieving a Pearson correlation of 0.79.

title: Taming Spatial Heterophily and Temporal Irregularity: A Curriculum Learning Approach for Traffic Forecasting
authors: Hongjun Wang, Zhiwen Zhang, Jiyuan Chen, Zipei Fan, Renhe Jiang, Wei Yuan, Ryosuke Shibasaki, Xuan Song
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: The advance in graph neural networks (GCNs) has propelled the development of spatial-temporal (ST) graph modeling (e.g., traffic flow prediction, and incident prediction) that intends to capture the inter-dependency between connected nodes and time sequence self-correlation simultaneously from dynamic node-level inputs. However, the existing literature trains their models with a random order, ignoring that the forecasting difficulty can vary significantly among node’s time sequences. Intuitively, the difficult samples indicate those patterns (e.g., abnormal patterns) are hard to learn by model directly. Thus, we consider that a curriculum with easy-to-difficult order, similar to the understanding procedure of humans, can improve the method’s performance in ST-graph modeling. Such an idea has a well-known name: curriculum learning (CL), which firstly designs the difficulty measurer for training samples and then feeds into the model with easy-to-difficult order according to the measurer. This paper explores the difficult time sequence whose temporal pattern is inconsistent with neighbors or has a poor auto-correlation. We design a spatial-temporal curriculum learning framework (STCL) trained by two additional ST tasks to obtain the vital power in ST imputation and measure each node’s signal difficulty corresponding to the performance in the auxiliary model in different ST scenarios. The ST-models are learned from scratch with explicit spatial affinities (subgraph) and easy temporal patterns, then progressively enrolled the nodes into the training dataset. Extensive experiments have been conducted in two public traffic flow datasets and two incident datasets, demonstrating our curriculum schedule’s superiority over without CL and other CL baselines.

title: FunDEMentals of Error-Shaping DEM DACs [Feature]
authors: Enrique Alvarez-Fontecilla, Paul S. Wilkins
publish: IEEE Circuits and Systems Magazine
abstract: DEM plays a key role in many systems such as high-performance <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Delta \Sigma $ </tex-math></inline-formula> ADCs and high-resolution Nyquist data converters. By randomizing DAC element selection, DEM transforms nonlinearity into noise, and in oversampled systems, it may additionally shape the noise’s spectrum to push its energy out of band. This tutorial paper focuses on error-shaping DEM. We review the basics, explain what shaped spectra means, and present practical errorshaping DEM encoder designs.

title: Jutted BMOCZ for Non-Coherent OFDM
authors: Parker Huggins, Alphan Şahin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: In this work, we propose a zero constellation for binary modulation on conjugate-reciprocal zeros (BMOCZ), called jutted BMOCZ (J-BMOCZ), and study its application to non-coherent orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM). With J-BMOCZ, we introduce rotational asymmetry to the zero constellation for Huffman BMOCZ, which removes ambiguity at the receiver under a uniform rotation of the zeros. The asymmetry is controlled by the magnitude of “jutted” zeros and enables the receiver to estimate zero rotation using a simple cross-correlation. The proposed method, however, leads to a natural trade-off between asymmetry and zero stability. Accordingly, we introduce a reliability metric to measure the stability of a polynomial’s zeros under an additive perturbation of the coefficients, and we apply the metric to optimize the J-BMOCZ zero constellation parameters. We then combine the advantages of J-BMOCZ and Huffman BMOCZ to design a hybrid waveform for OFDM with BMOCZ (OFDM-BMOCZ). The pilot-free waveform enables blind synchronization/detection and has a fixed peak-to-average power ratio that is independent of the message. Finally, we assess the proposed scheme through simulation and demonstrate non-coherent OFDM-BMOCZ using low-cost software-defined radios.

title: Dual Tensor Completion Network-Based Joint Frequency and DOA Estimation Under Spatio-Temporal Sub-Nyquist Sampling
authors: Xiaoming Zhi, Dawei Gao, Qinghua Guo, Xiaoning Liu, Yaxing Yue, Zilin Song, Guisheng Liao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: This paper presents a joint carrier frequency and two-dimensional (2-D) direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation method for spatio-temporal sub-Nyquist sampling systems equipped with sparse planar arrays. An identical-delay sub-Nyquist sampling architecture is adopted to couple temporal frequency information with spatial measurements, and the resulting observations are formulated as a third-order tensor with structured missing entries caused by sensor sparsity. To reliably recover this incomplete tensor, we propose an unsupervised dual tensor completion network (DCNet) that exploits the tensor's low-rank CANDECOMP/PARAFAC (CP) structure and spatial correlations. A physics-informed loss function, incorporating observation fidelity, phase consistency, and low-rank regularization, ensures that the completed tensor preserves the inherent array manifold structure. Based on the recovered full tensor, carrier frequencies and 2-D DOAs are jointly estimated via CP decomposition without requiring pairing operations. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves near full-array estimation accuracy and significantly outperforms existing tensor completion approaches, remaining effective even when a large fraction of sensors are randomly missing. These results indicate that the proposed framework enables accurate wideband spectrum sensing and spatial localization with significantly reduced hardware cost.

title: Role-based Adaptive Coordination for Resilient Signal Coverage in Stratospheric Balloons
authors: Zexin Wang, Zhenglin Liang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Superpressure balloons in the stratosphere are emerging as a promising platform for extending network connectivity to remote and disaster-affected regions. Yet, their dependence on altitude control and absence of horizontal maneuverability render them highly susceptible to wind drift, leading to unstable coverage. Coordinating multiple balloons further challenges current multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) methods, as wind-induced moderation effects can simultaneously disrupt all agents. Here we present a novel Adaptive Role-Switching Cooperative Learning (ARSCL), a dual-policy MARL framework that achieves resilient, cooperative coverage through adaptive role specialization. ARSCL assigns complementary roles —a main policy maintaining primary coverage and a compensatory policy introducing diversity to counteract correlated drift—linked by a dynamic role-switching mechanism that adapts to spatial deviation. A drift-prevention module further constrains learning to feasible trajectories. Simulations demonstrate that ARSCL achieves an average coverage duration exceeding 94% within a 25 km radius over a 24-hour period, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art MARL baselines in both efficiency and resilience.

title: Asymptotic Boundedness of Distributed Set-Membership Filtering
authors: Yudong Li, Yirui Cong, Shimin Wang, Martin Guay, Jiuxiang Dong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control
abstract: Asymptotic boundedness is a crucial property of Distributed Set-Membership Filtering (DSMFing) that prevents the unbounded growth of the set estimates caused by the wrapping effect. However, this important property remains underinvestigated, compared to its noise-free and stochastic-noise counterparts, i.e., the convergence of Distributed Observers (DOs) and the bounded error covariance of Distributed Kalman Filters (DKFs). This paper studies the asymptotic boundedness of DSMFing for linear discrete-time systems. A novel concept, termed the Distributed Observation-Information Tower (DOIT), is introduced to characterize the fundamental relationship between the structure of graphs and the set estimates, which enables the boundedness analysis. Leveraging the DOIT, an easily verifiable sufficient condition for the asymptotic boundedness of linear DSMFing is established. Surprisingly, the sufficient condition generalizes the well-known collective detectability condition for DOs and DKFs; it links DSMFs to existing distributed estimation methods and reveals the unique characteristic of DSMFs.

title: Synthetic Aperture Radar Image Change Detection Based on Global Dynamic Context-Aware Network
authors: Baogui Huan, Chuanzheng Gong, Dezhong Chen, Feng Gao, Junyu Dong, Qian Du
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have been extensively and successfully applied to the task of synthetic aperture radar (SAR) image change detection. However, conventional convolutional layers are inherently limited by their local receptive fields, which mainly capture spatially localized patterns while neglecting the global context that is often crucial for accurately distinguishing subtle or large-scale changes in SAR imagery. Moreover, developing robust and stable detection models in this domain remains particularly challenging due to the scarcity of annotated training samples, which frequently lead to overfitting and unstable convergence during training. To address these limitations, we propose a novel Global Dynamic Context-Aware Network (GDNet) specifically tailored for SAR image change detection. At the core of our approach lies a novel global dynamic convolution module, which adaptively modulates convolution kernel weights according to the global semantic information extracted from the input features. By dynamically incorporating long-range dependencies, this mechanism enables the network to integrate both local detail and global context, thus improving its ability to detect diverse change patterns. In addition, we intro duce a carefully designed two-stage Mixup strategy for model training. Unlike conventional single-stage Mixup, our two-stage design generates more diverse and informative training samples, effectively regularizing the model and yielding more stable and reliable classification results even under limited data scenarios. Extensive experiments on three SAR datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed GDNet compared to other state-of the-art methods. These findings highlight the potential of global dynamic modeling and advanced data augmentation strategies for advancing SAR image interpretation. Source codes are available at https://github.com/oucailab/GDNet.

title: ASAP: A 28-nm Transformer Training Accelerator With Alternating Sparsity and Asymmetrical Microscaling Precision
authors: HanGyeol Mun, Jian Meng, Xiaofeng Hu, Yuan Liao, Chun-Ting Chen, Jae-sun Seo
publish: IEEE Journal of Solid-State Circuits
abstract: This work presents ASAP, a 28-nm transformer-training accelerator that combines N:M structured sparsity with asymmetric microscaling floating-point (MXFP) precision through a unified algorithm–hardware co-design. ASAP introduces a progressive sparsity schedule in which pruned compute resources are reassigned to increase numerical precision for important weights and activations, stabilizing optimization while reducing overall computation. A placement-aware N:M reordering mechanism aligns partial products within each processing element, minimizing shift distances, lowering toggle activity, and sustaining utilization across sparsity levels. To address the performance bottleneck of nonlinear (NL) layers, a reconfigurable pipeline single-instruction multiple-data (SIMD) engine supports various NL functions and their gradients through a scatter-network datapath, achieving up to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$3.4\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> throughput improvement. Fabricated in 28-nm CMOS, ASAP attains 22.4 TOPS/W peak efficiency and delivers 2.02–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$5.07\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> higher end-to-end training energy efficiency than prior training processors, while preserving FP32-level accuracy on MAE ViT-B and BERT workloads. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of coordinated sparsity–precision scheduling and placement-aware dataflow for future energy-efficient transformer-training accelerators.

title: Low-Crosstalk 16-Channel Second-Order Microring WDM Filter and Application to a Wavelength-Selective Switch Unit
authors: Xing Yu, Yangming Ren, Xinyu Li, Shenghang Zhou, Houyou Lai, Zhiwei Kong, Binhao Wang, Qian Chen, Xiubao Sui, Wenfu Zhang
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: A compact second-order microring filter based on optimized dual-Bezier bends is designed and experimentally validated on a silicon-on-insulator platform. This work presents the experimentally demonstrated high-performance 16-channel dense wavelength-division multiplexing device based on dual-ring filters, featuring a channel spacing of 200 GHz. Experimental characterization reveals insertion losses ranging from 0.3 to 2.9 dB, adjacent channel crosstalks suppressed below -22.1 dB, and 3-dB bandwidths of approximately 100 GHz. To further address the critical issues of optical crosstalk induced by extinction ratio degradation at the through port of high-order microring wavelength-selective switches (WSS), a 1<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\times 2\times 8\lambda$</tex-math></inline-formula> WSS unit is proposed and demonstrated. Compared with conventional drop-through WSS architectures, the proposed dual-drop-port WSS unit achieves crosstalk levels below -20 dB for all channels. Optical communication experiment validation is performed via 30 Gbps non-return-to-zero on-off keying (NRZ-OOK) dual-channel modulated signals. The proposed scheme exhibits superior eye diagram quality and enhanced interference immunity compared to traditional WSS unit. These results provide a robust and scalable building block for the realization of large-scale, multi-wavelength on-chip high-order microring optical switching networks and high-capacity optical interconnects. A compact second-order microring filter based on optimized dual-Bezier bends is designed and experimentally validated on a silicon-on-insulator platform. This work presents the experimentally demonstrated high-performance 16-channel dense wavelength-division multiplexing device based on dual-ring filters, featuring a channel spacing of 200 GHz. Experimental characterization reveals insertion losses ranging from 0.3 to 2.9 dB, adjacent channel crosstalks suppressed below -22.1 dB, and 3-dB bandwidths of approximately 100 GHz. To further address the critical issues of optical crosstalk induced by extinction ratio degradation at the through port of high-order microring wavelength-selective switches (WSS), a 1<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\times 2\times 8\lambda$</tex-math></inline-formula> WSS unit is proposed and demonstrated. Compared with conventional drop-through WSS architectures, the proposed dual-drop-port WSS unit achieves crosstalk levels below -20 dB for all channels. Optical communication experiment validation is performed via 30 Gbps non-return-to-zero on-off keying (NRZ-OOK) dual-channel modulated signals. The proposed scheme exhibits superior eye diagram quality and enhanced interference immunity compared to traditional WSS unit. These results provide a robust and scalable building block for the realization of large-scale, multi-wavelength on-chip high-order microring optical switching networks and high-capacity optical interconnects.

title: Engineering LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems: A Taxonomy of Emerging Frameworks
authors: Davide Di Ruscio, Phuong T. Nguyen, Claudio Di Sipio, Riccardo Rubei, Juri Di Rocco
publish: IEEE Software
abstract: LLM–based multi-agent systems (LMAS) are emerging as a promising paradigm, enabling specialized agents to collaborate and act autonomously across complex software engineering (SE) tasks. Yet, despite rapid progress, the field still lacks a clear conceptual foundation to guide researchers and practitioners in systematically designing, implementing, and evaluating such systems. This article introduces a taxonomy intended to provide practical guidance for integrating LMAS into software development workflows. Grounded in an analysis of prominent frameworks and scientific contributions for engineering LMAS, the taxonomy organizes the design space along five key dimensions offering a structured lens for understanding and engineering LMAS. Our analysis reveals substantial gaps in current frameworks, particularly regarding monitoring, performance evaluation, and quality assessment. These findings highlight pressing research challenges and identify concrete directions toward more reliable, transparent, and effective agentic systems for SE.

title: Unified Metric Learning for Personalized Fashion Recommendations with Fast User Adaptation
authors: Zhi Lu, Yang Hu, Cong Yu, Yan Chen, Bing Zeng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Personlized fashion recommendation aims to model outfit composition quality and recommend compatible item combinations for users. However, existing studies often address outfit-level and item-level recommendations as separate problems, resulting in fragmented formulations. In this work, we revisit these problems from a unified mathematical perspective and extend fashion recommendation into a coherent family of composition tasks. These tasks, including outfit recommendation, outfit completion, outlier item detection, and new user profiling, are formulated within a common probabilistic framework that differs only in search constraints or conditioning. Based on this unified view, we propose a metric learning framework that embeds items, outfits, and users into a shared space where outfit compatibility is modeled as the probability of co-occurrence under composition utility. The learned embeddings provide consistent evaluation across tasks, enable personalized recommendation, and support fast user adaptation through plug-and-play user parameters without fine-tuning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple composition tasks and establishes a unified foundation for generalizable and adaptive fashion recommendation.

title: Accurate Protein Submitochondrial Localization Using Pre-Training and Adaptive Sampling Enhanced Deep Learning Model
authors: Ting Zhu, Yuanyuan Liu, Guosheng Han
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Mitochondria consist of four primary submitochondrial compartments: the matrix, outer membrane, inner membrane, and intermembrane space. However, current research has generated relatively limited experimental data on the intermembrane space, which constrains the comprehensive understanding of its biological functions. Furthermore, existing methods for predicting protein submitochondrial localization predominantly rely on machine learning approaches that typically require expert level biological knowledge for manual feature engineering and fail to adequately address the class imbalance issue. To address these limitations, this paper proposes an end-to-end deep learning model iPreDeepSubMito for protein submitochondrial localization. The core contribution of our model lies in three aspects: (1) a better pre-trained protein model is utilized for enhanced sequence embedding; (2) The adaptive synthetic sampling approach is seamlessly incorporated into the model to handle imbalanced datasets; and (3) the recurrent residual convolutional neural networks (rrCNNs) and a self-attention enhanced bidirectional gated recurrent unit (saBiGRU) are newly constructed and integrated to effectively capture contextual semantic features of protein sequences. Experimental results on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that iPreDeepSubMito consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing methods by 10-26% in overall General Correlation Coefficient (GCC) and delivering 16-60% higher Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) for the challenging intermembrane space class. In addition, extensive ablation experiments on core modules validate the effectiveness and advancement of the proposed approach. Therefore, this study improves the performance of protein submitochondrial localization, which could provide valuable insights into disease pathogenesis and facilitate advances in drug design.

title: A Synergy-Coherence Integration Method for Real-Time Knee Contact Force Estimation under Multiple Gait Patterns
authors: Yibo Han, Xianzhong Li, Di Shi, Yanggang Feng, Xilun Ding, Wuxiang Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Accurate real-time estimation of medial knee contact force (KCF) is essential for knee osteoarthritis research and rehabilitation assessment. To overcome the limitations of existing methods such as numerous sensors requirements and insufficient robustness, this study proposes a novel real-time medial KCF estimation framework that integrates muscle synergies with intermuscular coherence, enabling high-accuracy estimation using five EMG sensors across five gait patterns. First, muscle synergy extraction is employed to select five key muscles for dimensionality reduction. Second, intermuscular coherence is computed to characterize neuromuscular coordination under different gait conditions. These complementary temporal and frequency domain features are subsequently integrated and processed through a dual-branch LSTM network for contact force prediction. Evaluation on a public dataset demonstrated strong performance (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.87 ± 0.07) across five gait conditions, exceeding baseline methods, while leave-one-gait-out testing further confirmed the method's superior generalization and robustness. Real-world validation with 12 participants, combined with EMG-informed musculoskeletal modeling, demonstrated that the real-time peak force estimation errors were no more than 0.18 BW (body-weight) during walking tests across five gait patterns. This study provides a practical and robust solution for real-time knee force monitoring with fewer sensors across multiple gait conditions, offering significant potential for applications in motor function assessment, personalized rehabilitation interventions, and integration with intelligent assistive devices.

title: Towards the Design and Development of Intelligent Transportation Devices
authors: Muhammad Mudasser Latif Minhas, Nofal Elahi, Rehan Ahmed, Junaid Arshad, Rehan Hafiz, Muhammad Adnan Siddique, Imran Sabir, Muhammad Zaman
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Road traffic accidents, congestion, and inefficient mobility cause significant loss of life, time, and economic productivity worldwide, driving growing demand for Intelligent Transportation System (ITS). However, high cost of ITS impedes its wide-scale adoption. There is a need for low-cost, open source Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I) hardware to enable wide research adoption and real-world deployment. This paper presents the complete design, implementation, and field evaluation of an experimental On Board Unit (OBU) and Road Side Unit (RSU) for V2I communication. Both devices are built from commercial off-the-shelf components. The unit cost is approximately $117 (OBU) and $80 (RSU). This is substantially lower compared to commercial Dedicated Short Range Communication (DSRC) based devices. Point-to-point range tests demonstrated minimum packet delivery rate of 81% at distances up to 485m under obstructed, non-line-of-sight conditions. A two-OBU, two-RSU testbed was subsequently deployed at a University Campus. Field measurements confirmed the feasibility of the developed design as a low-cost V2I research/testbed platform. OBU⇌RSU round-trip latency was observed to be less than 300 ms. This makes the proposed design suitable for prototype V2I applications such as signal advisory, road-hazard alerts, and traffic monitoring. The Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR) for the nominal OBU–RSU pair was observed to be 78% for OBU→RSU and 73% for RSU→OBU. This demonstrates consistent low-latency communication for prototype V2I applications.

title: PACE - Plantard Accelerated CRYSTALS Extensions
authors: R. Bevin, Seogchung Seo, A. Khalid, M. O’Neill
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: ML-KEM (FIPS203) and ML-DSA (FIPS204) were two of the first post quantum cryptography schemes to be standardized by the National Institute of Standards and Technology; Both CRYSTALS algorithms utilize modulo reduction, specifically Montgomery reduction.
